items.title: Enable 2FA in pgAdmin 4 (Server Mode) — TOTP & Email OTP with Docker
items.author: Worapon Asavanik
items.content:
Enable 2FA in pgAdmin 4 (Server Mode) — TOTP & Email OTP with Docker

Set up 2FA in pgAdmin 4 (Server Mode) running in Docker. This guide covers both TOTP (Google/Microsoft Authenticator) and Email OTP via Gmail SMTP.

In this guide, we’ll enable two types of 2FA in pgAdmin 4 (Server Mode):, which can be used immediately by scanning a QR code with Google or Microsoft Authenticator, and , which requires additional setup.

For Email OTP, we’ll configure a and modify both config.py and config_local.py to enable and customize multi-factor authentication.While config.py contains the main settings, config_local.py is used to safely override specific configurations without modifying the core system file — which is useful in containerized environments.

Example configurations include enabling MFA, customizing the email subject, and setting up SMTP credentials such as:

MFA_ENABLED = TrueMFA_FORCE_REGISTRATION = False
MAIL_SERVER = 'smtp.gmail.com'
MAIL_PORT = 465MAIL_USERNAME = 'your_email@gmail.com'
MAIL_PASSWORD = 'your_app_password'
This article covers:1.Quick overview of 2FA — What it is and why it’s important for pgAdmin security3.TOTP: Enable TOTP authentication — Set up login via authenticator apps (no extra config needed)Prepare Gmail SMTP credentials (App Password) for pgAdmin — Make sure pgAdmin has 2FA support enabled— Set email-related settings like SMTP credentials7.Summary of All Commands

Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) adds an extra layer of security to your pgAdmin login by requiring a second form of verification, typically beyond just a password.

3.TOTP: Enable TOTP authentication

Start by accessing the pgAdmin 4 server via http://<host_ip>:<pgadmin_port> in your browser. Then, log in using the username and password you defined in the docker-compose.yml file.

After accessing the pgAdmin dashboard, look at the top-right corner and click on your username.

A small dropdown menu will appear — click on Two-Factor Authentication.

A popup window will open. In the section, click .

You will be redirected to a small window showing a QR code and a field below it to enter the TOTP. At this point, just use your phone with an authenticator app (such as Google Authenticator or Microsoft Authenticator) to scan the code. Then, enter the 6-digit code that appears (it refreshes every 30 seconds) and click the  button.

After clicking the Continue button, you’ve successfully set up Two-Factor Authentication using the Authenticator App!

Let’s now test if the authentication works correctly by logging out and logging back in again.

Now, you may notice that previously, after entering a valid username and password, you were taken directly to the pgAdmin dashboard.

However, after enabling Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) with an authenticator app, logging in with valid credentials (username and password) will now prompt you to enter your TOTP.

Enter the 6-digit code from your authenticator app — just like you did during setup.

If the TOTP is valid, clicking the Validate button will log you in and redirect you to the dashboard as usual.

To enable Email-based OTP in pgAdmin 4, you need to configure an  — this allows pgAdmin to send one-time codes to users via email.

In this example, we use as the . Gmail does allow direct use of your account password for external apps. Instead, it allows you to generate a secure that acts as a special key for SMTP access.

Once you’ve successfully created the App Password, you’ll get a 16-character code as shown below (this is what you’ll use in your config later).

Once you’ve generated your Gmail App Password, just keep it somewhere safe — we’ll use it in the next step when configuring config_local.py.

Let’s move on to setting up the configuration.

In this section, we’re going to configure the config.py file. How you do this depends on your environment and preference.

If you’re running pgAdmin inside a Debian- or Ubuntu-based container, you can use the CLI directly by running:

docker exec -it <your_pgadmin_container_name> sh

Inside the container, you should be able to use vi or vim to edit the file.

However, since I’m using , I don’t have access to a full-featured shell inside the container.So I had to copy the file out, edit it on the host machine, and then copy it back into the container.

config.py directly inside the container, feel free to skip the following steps.
Just make sure the file includes the following parameters:MFA_SUPPORTED_METHODS = ["email", "authenticator"], as shown in the picture below.
Accessing and copying files from the container

To open a shell inside your pgAdmin container, run:

docker exec -it <your_container_name> sh# in my case:
docker exec -it chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1 sh

To copy the config.py file from inside the container to your host machine, use:

docker cp <your_container_name>:/<path_inside_container> <destination_on_host>/config.py# in my case:
docker cp chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1:/pgadmin4/config.py config.py

After successfully copying the file to your host machine, open it in your preferred text editor.

The file is quite long, so scroll down and look for the following section:

Two-Factor Authentication Configuration

Make sure the following parameters are correctly set:

MFA_ENABLED = TrueMFA_SUPPORTED_METHODS = ["email", "authenticator"]

Once you’ve verified or updated the settings, copy the file back into the container:

docker cp <local_file> <container_name>:<container_path>/config.py# in my case:
docker cp config.py chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1:/pgadmin4/config.py

OK, after we’ve finished configuring the config.py, let's move on to the next step — creating the config_local.py file.

The config_local.py file is used to override values in config.py without disrupting the base configuration.

Just like earlier, if your container has a shell editor like nano, vi, or vim, you can create and edit the file directly inside the container using:

touch config_local.pyvi config_local.py

But in my case, since I’m using Docker Desktop (and Alpine doesn’t include nano or vim by default, I believe), I'll create the file using Notepad on my host machine and save it with a .py extension.

config_local.py file contains the following configuration:
###########################################################################################MFA_ENABLED = True
MFA_FORCE_REGISTRATION = False
# Default: <APP_NAME> - Verification Code
MFA_EMAIL_SUBJECT = None
###########################################################################################
MAIL_SERVER = 'smtp.gmail.com'MAIL_USE_SSL = True
MAIL_USERNAME = 'woraponasvn36@gmail.com'
MAIL_PASSWORD = 'xxxx xxxx xxxx xxxx'
###########################################################################################
  • → The SMTP server address for Gmail. This value is fixed and should not be changed.
  • → Port used for SMTPS (SMTP over SSL).

    → This is Gmail’s default port for encrypted SSL connections.

    → Cannot be changed if MAIL_USE_SSL = True.
  • → Enables SSL encryption for the connection.

    → Required when using port 465 with Gmail.

  • → Your Gmail address that will be used to send OTP emails.

    → Must match the account used to generate the App Password.

  • → The 16-digit App Password generated from your Gmail account.

    → Used instead of your actual Gmail login password.

run docker cp config_local.py chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1:/pgadmin4/config_local.py

Now let’s check that config_local.py has been placed in the correct location.

Run the following command to open a shell inside the container:

docker exec -it chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1 sh

OK, now that config_local.py is in place inside the pgAdmin container, the only thing left to do is to apply the changes.

Run the following command:

sudo docker restart <your_container_id># In my case:

Once restarted, access pgAdmin again via:

http://<your_host_ip>:<pgadmin_port>

Log in as usual. Then, at the top-right corner of the dashboard, click your account name and select Two-Factor Authentication.

This time, we’ll proceed to set up Email-based authentication

After clicking the button, you’ll be redirected to a small window.

In the field labeled “Enter the email address to send a code”, enter the email address where you want the OTP to be sent.

Typically, this should match the MAIL_USERNAME you defined in your config_local.py file.

After clicking the button, you’ll be redirected to a new pop-up window asking for a verification code.

Check your email for the OTP that was just sent, enter the code, and click to complete the setup.

This confirms that the email settings in config_local.py are working correctly — the OTP has been delivered.

After clicking the button, a small pop-up will appear in the top-right corner, as shown in the image above, with the message:“Email Authentication registered successfully”

At this point, you’ve successfully set up both TOTP and Email OTP for Two-Factor Authentication.

Let’s now test that Email OTP is working properly by logging out and logging back in again.

After setting up Email Authentication, you can now choose between both authentication methods, as shown in the following picture.

Once you enter the OTP sent to your email, you’ll be successfully logged in to the pgAdmin dashboard.

7.Summary of All Commands

docker exec -it <your_pgadmin_container_name> shdocker exec -it <your_container_name> shdocker exec -it chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1 sh
docker cp <your_container_name>:/<path_inside_container> <destination_on_host>/config.py
# in my case:
docker cp chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1:/pgadmin4/config.py config.py
docker cp <local_file> <container_name>:<container_path>/config.pydocker cp config.py chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1:/pgadmin4/config.pyvi config_local.py
run docker cp config_local.py chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1:/pgadmin4/config_local.py
docker exec -it chiccrmdb-pgadmin-1 sh
sudo docker restart <your_container_id>
# In my case:

Have a nice day!Every clap means a lot and motivates me to keep sharing.

Love you all, readers. Bye-bye! 💙

items.title: UNDERSTANDING BASICS OF KUBERNETES
items.author: Naman Sharma
items.content:
Kubernetes: The Basics Explained

Before delving into the concept of Kubernetes, let us first gain an understanding through a practical example, after which we will proceed with a detailed explanation.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────|                  Kubernetes Restaurant Cluster       |
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
|                      MASTER NODE                     |
|              (Cook / Head Chef - Control Plane)      |
|                                                      |
|  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ |
|  |              API Server (Restaurant Manager)    | |
|  | - Takes orders (kubectl commands)               | |
|  | - Communicates with all other components        | |
|  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ |
|                                                      |
|  ┌──────────────────────────┐ ┌────────────────────┐ |
|  | Scheduler (Table Planner)| | Controller Manager | |
|  | - Assigns tasks to       | | - Ensures orders   | |
|  |   available waiters      | |   stay active      | |
|  └──────────────────────────┘ └────────────────────┘ |
|                                                      |
|  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ |
|  | etcd (Recipe Book)                              | |
|  | - Stores menu, staff, and config data           | |
|  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ |
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
                         ||────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
|                    WORKER NODES (Waiters)            |
|                                                      |
| ┌─────────────────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ |
| | Node 1                      | | Node 2           | |
| | - Waiter executes orders    | | - Another waiter | |
| | - Runs pods (customers)     | | - More pods      | |
| |                             | |                  | |
| | ┌────────────────────────┐  | | ┌──────────────┐ | |
| | | Pod (Customer)         |  | | | Pod          | | |
| | | ┌────────────────────┐ |  | | | ┌──────────┐ | | |
| | | | Container (Meal)   | |  | | | | Container| | | |
| | | └────────────────────┘ |  | | └────────────┘ | | |
| | └────────────────────────┘  | └──────────────────┘ |
| └─────────────────────────────┘                      |
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
                         ||────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
|              Deployment = Menu Configuration         |
|  - Describes how many meals (replicas) to prepare    |
|  - Defines what ingredients (containers/images) to use|
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Kubernetes is the container orchestration tool which helps in managing the containers , scaling them , rollback , handling failure ,load balancing and recovering containers efficiently. Let’s assume we have five containers. Managing them manually especially during failures can be very difficult.

For example, if one or more containers crash, you would have to restart them individually.

solves this problem by automating container management. It can automatically detect failed containers and restart them, scale containers up or down based on load, and even perform rolling updates or rollbacks without downtime. It also provides and self-healing capabilities, making containerized applications much more resilient and easier to manage.

cluster is a set of machines(virtual or physical) running Kubernetes, working together to run containerized applications.

  • A (also called the ), which manages the cluster, schedules workloads, and makes decisions (like scaling, updates, etc.).
  • One or more , which are responsible for actually running the applications.
  • Inside , you have .
  • Inside , you have one or more .

What is a Pod in Kubernetes?

  1. A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. It represents a single instance of a running process in your cluster.
  2. A pod consists of mainly one container can also consists of more than one.(multi-container pod)
  3. If there are more than one containers in the Pod, they share the same volume and network resources.

What is the Namespace in Kubernetes?

  1. Logical separation inside our cluster to manage the resources.
  2. Used to separate environment such as prod, test and dev.
  3. By default in Kubernetes we have three namespace: default, kube-system and kube-public.

Kubernetes Cluster Overview

1. Master Node (Control Plane)

Responsible for managing the Kubernetes cluster and maintaining the desired state.

  • : Handles all REST API requests to the Kubernetes cluster. It’s the entry point for all administrative tasks.
  • : A key-value store that stores the entire state of the cluster (e.g., pod states, configs).
  • : Assigns Pods to Nodes based on resource availability and policies.
  • 1.4 kube-controller-manager: Watches the state of the cluster via the API server and makes changes to achieve the desired state (e.g., starting or stopping Pods).
  • 1.5 cloud-controller-manager: Integrates with cloud provider APIs (e.g., for load balancers, storage, node management).

Runs the application workloads.

  • : Communicates with the control plane, registers the node, and ensures containers in pods are running.
  • : Manages network rules and handles routing of traffic to the appropriate pods.
  • : The software responsible for running containers (e.g., Docker, containerd).
  • 2.4.1 Pod 1 → Container(s)
  • 2.4.2 Pod 2 → Container(s)

A is an abstraction that defines a logical set of Pods and a policy by which to access them.

  • It is used to expose the application internally or externally (e.g., ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer).

is a tool that allows you to run on your personal machine for development and testing purposes.

  • It creates a single-node Kubernetes cluster.
  • This single node acts as both the Master Node and the Worker Node.
  • We are using Minikube in this project to get a basic understanding of how Kubernetes works. Since setting up a full Kubernetes cluster requires more resources (like at least 2 CPUs an multiple nodes), Minikube provides a lightweight, single-node cluster that runs locally. It helps simulate a real Kubernetes environment for learning and testing without the need for complex infrastructure.

Let us see how we can use Minikube locally.

: If you are having a Linux operating system that's good. Install docker in it and minikube. If you are having windows operating system install WSL and Docker for windows.

Clone an exisiting Project from Github or create your own.

git clone https://github.com/namanupmanyu/DevopsApp.git
#this is the sample project that I have created to understand complete Devops workflow.

Start the minikube service. This will create a single-node Kubernetes cluster running inside a Docker container.

minikube start --driver=docker
#donot forget to start the docker service also.

: Create a deployment.yml file which is already present in the project.

minikube kubectl -- apply -f deployment.yml
minikube kubectl get pods
#in the below mentioned screenshot i have used a command i.e. 
minikube kubectl delete deployment ruby-sample-deployment
#used this beacuse i have already created a deployment.

Step 5: Create a service.yml file which is already present in the project.

minikube kubectl -- apply -f service.yml
minikube service <servicename>

: Access your application.

Note: Same steps we need to follow when we are using K8s.

As we can see there is a little bug in our application and we need some changes. In my project, I have already create a readme.md file which tell us how to run the project. Let us make changes and apply them.

: I have connect to the ec2 instance which was created using Terraform. Further We can see that Jenkins is already installed Docker is already installed this is because I have used ansible a configuration management tool.

Let us now connect to the Jenkins on port 8080.

Step 3: Lets us now make changes in our index.html page then after committing those change let us push the changes to the Github.

cd devopsappcd app/views/homegit commit -m "commit message"
#remeber to install git and create your own github account.

Step 4: Let us now build our pipeline manually. To build automatically we have to add webhooks.(refer to the readme.md)

Let us now access our application and see docker container is being created and is running or not.

Step 6: Remember we were using minikube for deployment now let us repeat all the steps that I have mentioned earlier.

minikube start --driver=docker
minikube kubectl -- apply -f deployment.yml
minikube kubectl get pods
minikube kubectl -- apply -f service.yml
minikube service  dev-app-service

That wraps up our understanding of the DevOps lifecycle for now. There are still a few parts we haven’t covered, and I’ll try to include them in my next blog.

Thank you for reading…………

items.title: How to Deploy Amazon EKS Auto Mode with Custom Node Pools and Node classes Using Terraform
items.author: Manusha Pamoda
items.content:
Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) Auto Mode is a fully managed deployment option that streamlines running containerized applications at scale. It eliminates the operational overhead by handling infrastructure provisioning, cluster management, and performance optimization enabling developers to focus purely on application deployment and execution.

In contrast to traditional EKS clusters, which require manual management of node groups and networking configurations, EKS Auto Mode simplifies the process by abstracting these complexities. The result is a seamless, cost-efficient, and secure Kubernetes experience.

If we look at the EKS auto mode cluster architecture, we can see how much of change auto mode has implemented compare to previous EKS cluster architecture.

Reasons to Use Custom Node Pools and Node Classes

When you create a cluster with EKS Auto Mode, it give you the option to use two built-in managed node pools — General Purpose and System — along with . While these built-in managed node pools simplify application deployment, they lack the flexibility needed for custom configurations.

If you inspect the YAML configuration for the General Purpose node pool, you’ll notice that the instance families (c, m, r) are hardcoded into the setup:

- key: eks.amazonaws.com/instance-categoryoperator: In- c- r

While this default configuration works well for general workloads, it limits your ability to optimize for specific use cases.

Why Choose Custom Node Pools?

To leverage instance categories that are better suited for your applications, creating a custom node pool is the way to go. With custom node pools, you can select the instance types that best match your workload’s performance and cost requirements, without being restricted to the predefined categories.

Additionally, although you can use the default node class with your custom node pool, EKS also allows you to create entirely new node classes, giving you even more control over how your workloads are managed and optimized.

Terraform Setup for Amazon EKS in Auto Mode

resource "aws_eks_cluster" "example" {  name = "example"    authentication_mode = "API"
    bootstrap_cluster_creator_admin_permissions = true
  }
  role_arn = aws_iam_role.cluster.arn
  version  = "1.32"
  bootstrap_self_managed_addons = false
    enabled       = true  }
  kubernetes_network_config {
    elastic_load_balancing {    }    block_storage {    }    support_type = "STANDARD"    endpoint_private_access = true
    endpoint_public_access  = true
      aws_subnet.az1.id,      aws_subnet.az3.id,  }    aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.cluster_AmazonEKSClusterPolicy,
    aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.cluster_AmazonEKSComputePolicy,
    aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.cluster_AmazonEKSBlockStoragePolicy,
    aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.cluster_AmazonEKSLoadBalancingPolicy,
    aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.cluster_AmazonEKSNetworkingPolicy,}
resource "aws_iam_role" "node" {
  name = "eks-auto-node-example"
  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"      {
        Action = ["sts:AssumeRole"]
        Effect = "Allow"          Service = "ec2.amazonaws.com"      },  })
resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "node_AmazonEKSWorkerNodeMinimalPolicy" {
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEKSWorkerNodeMinimalPolicy"
  role       = aws_iam_role.node.name
}
resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "node_AmazonEC2ContainerRegistryPullOnly" {
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEC2ContainerRegistryPullOnly"
  role       = aws_iam_role.node.name
}
resource "aws_iam_role" "cluster" {
  name = "eks-cluster-example"
  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"      {          "sts:AssumeRole",        ]        Principal = {
          Service = "eks.amazonaws.com"
        }    ]}
resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "cluster_AmazonEKSClusterPolicy" {
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEKSClusterPolicy"
  role       = aws_iam_role.cluster.name
}
resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "cluster_AmazonEKSComputePolicy" {
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEKSComputePolicy"
  role       = aws_iam_role.cluster.name
}
resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "cluster_AmazonEKSBlockStoragePolicy" {
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEKSBlockStoragePolicy"
  role       = aws_iam_role.cluster.name
}
resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "cluster_AmazonEKSLoadBalancingPolicy" {
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEKSLoadBalancingPolicy"
  role       = aws_iam_role.cluster.name
}
resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "cluster_AmazonEKSNetworkingPolicy" {
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEKSNetworkingPolicy"
  role       = aws_iam_role.cluster.name
}

When using EKS Auto Mode these configurations are .

compute_config.enabled = truekubernetes_network_config.elastic_load_balancing.enabled = true
storage_config.block_storage.enabled = true
bootstrap_self_managed_addons = false

Create Custom Node Pools and Node Classes

apiVersion: karpenter.sh/v1kind: NodePool  name: custompool  disruption:    - nodes: 10%    consolidationPolicy: WhenEmptyOrUnderutilized    spec:      nodeClassRef:        kind: NodeClass      requirements:
      - key: karpenter.sh/capacity-type
        operator: In        - on-demand
      - key: node.kubernetes.io/instance-type
        operator: In        - "t3.medium"
      - key: kubernetes.io/arch
        operator: In        - amd64
      terminationGracePeriod: 24h0m0s
apiVersion: eks.amazonaws.com/v1kind: NodeClass  name: customclass  ephemeralStorage:    size: "80Gi"  networkPolicy: DefaultAllow
  networkPolicyEventLogs: Disabled
  role: NonProd-EKS-auto-node-Role
  securityGroupSelectorTerms:
  - id: "sg-1"  subnetSelectorTerms:  - id: "subnet-2"

In the template I have created a custom node pool with the t3.medium instance type. According to the AWS documentation, each instance type supports a specific maximum number of pods. For example, a t3.medium instance can host up to 15 pods.

Key Considerations for Custom Node Pools

When configuring custom node pools in EKS, it’s crucial to understand the limitations of EKS Auto Mode. Specifically, the following labels are in Auto Mode which we are supported in the regular EKS mode:

node.kubernetes.io/windows-buildkubernetes.io/os

This is because EKS Auto Mode exclusively supports Linux environments. It leverages , an open-source, Linux-based operating system purpose-built by AWS for running containers. Bottlerocket is optimized for security and minimalism, containing only the essential software required to efficiently run container workloads.

By keeping these factors in mind, you can ensure your custom node pools are configured correctly and optimized for performance in EKS.

AWS EKS Auto Mode simplifies Kubernetes infrastructure to the point where anyone without much prior experience in Kubernetes can deploy workloads. By abstracting away complex components EKS Auto Mode allows developers to focus more on building and deploying applications rather than managing the underlying infrastructure. As Kubernetes adoption continues to grow, this will be bring more users towards using Kubernetes.

items.title: Build a Scalable Serverless API with AWS Console— Real Project Walkthrough
items.author: Mohamed Aymn
items.content:
Build a Scalable Serverless API with AWS Console— Real Project Walkthrough

💡 Read this article for free: Here’s the friend link — no login or membership required!

In this guide, I’ll walk you through building a fully serverless backend for a shoe e-commerce website using AWS services:

  • AWS Cognito for authentication
  • API Gateway for REST APIs
  • Lambda for serverless compute
  • DynamoDB for NoSQL databases

Serverless architecture offers several compelling advantages:

  • : Focus on code, not infrastructure
  • : Costs scale with your traffic
  • : Handle traffic spikes with ease
  • Built-in high availability: AWS ensures redundancy across zones

Real-World Example of How Serverless Benefits This Project?

  • : You’d need to set up EC2 instances, load balancers, and databases, and manually scale them.
  • : AWS handles scaling and infrastructure, allowing you to focus on API design, security, and UX.

Here’s how our AWS components fit together:

  • : Routes HTTP requests to specific Lambda functions
  • : Runs backend logic (e.g., fetching shoe data, processing orders)
  • : Stores product details, user orders, and inventory
  • : Manages user authentication and secure API access

Critical AWS Best Practices

🚨 : Never use the root user for AWS projects. AWS follows the principle of least privilege, meaning you should only grant the minimum permissions needed for a task.

📸 Screenshots are included below to help you set this up step by step!

Step 1: Setting Up an IAM User

Skip this if you already have an IAM user with appropriate permissions.

An IAM user is an entity in your AWS account representing a person or system interacting with AWS resources. Unlike the root user, an IAM user can have limited permissions, which is recommended for security. Learn more from the AWS docs.

  1. Sign in using the root user and head to the IAM console
  2. Choose “Users” and click “”

3. For the , enter something like:

  • YourName-IAM-Admin (e.g., Mohamed-IAM-Admin)
  • “Provide user access to the AWS Management Console (optional)”
  • If asked, “Are you providing access to a person?”, select “I want to create an IAM user.”

5. Set console login credentials:

  • Choose “Custom password.”
  • Deselect “Users must create a new password at next sign-in.”

6. Assign administrator permissions:

  • Choose “Attach policies directly.”
  • Select “AdministratorAccess”

On the page, choose .

From the list, select:

Click , then .

Now you’ve successfully created your IAM Admin user!

7. Download credentials (optional):

  • Stay on the User Created page.
  • Click “Download .csv file” (it contains sign-in URL, username, and password)
  • Copy the Console Sign-in URL for easy access later

8. Log in as Your New IAM User

Now, you’re logged in as your IAM Admin User!

Step 2: Setting Up Amazon Cognito User Pool

What is Cognito and Why Use It?

Cognito provides authentication, authorization, and user management for your applications. It’s like having “Sign in with Google” functionality but for your own app, completely free.

Understanding Cognito Components:

  • User pools (Authentication): Manages sign-up, sign-in, supports social logins, and provides JWT tokens
  • Identity pools (Authorization): Grants temporary AWS credentials for accessing AWS services
User Pools = Who can sign in? (Authentication) Identity Pools = What can they access? (Authorization)

1. Head to the Cognito service from within the AWS Console

2. Choose “User Pools” from the sidebar(Authentication service)

3. Click “Create User Pool”

4. Select “Traditional web application” and give your application a name

5. Configure authentication settings:

  • Choose sign-in identifiers (Email, Phone Number, Username)
  • Select the required attributes for sign-up

Options for Sign-In Identifiers

  • If only is selected, users must pick one as their .
  • If a is included, users can sign in with .

💡 Cannot be changed after app creation!

Required Attributes for Sign-Up

Determines what users must provide during registration.

  • Common required attributes: email, phone number, name, and address.
  • If using a , an must also be required.

We won’t need a return URL in our application for simplicity purposes, but if you’re curious about what a return URL is. Basically, it specifies where users will be directed after a successful authentication

After following along in creating user pools in Cognito. You should see that you have successfully created your user pool and access your sign-in page

6. Complete the setup and test the sign-up/sign-in functionality

7. Verify users in your user pool by checking Users under “User Management.”

We have our user. Isn’t that amazing?

Step 3: Creating a DynamoDB Table

Now that users can sign up and sign in, we need a database to store user information:

  1. Go to the DynamoDB service

2. Click “Create table” and give it a name (e.g., “Users”)

3. Set the Partition key to “userId” with a string value type

4. Keep the default settings and click “Create table.”

Step 4: Creating a Lambda Function to Store Users

Now that we have our table, users can sign up successfully. We need a way to write our users to our users' table. And we are doing this through Lambda.

lambda is code-as-a-service from aws

We need a way to write users to our DynamoDB table when they sign up:

  1. Head to Lambda’s dashboard and create a function

2. Select “Author from scratch”

3. Give your Lambda function a name (e.g., “StoreUsersInTable”)

4. Select a runtime (Python 3.13 in this example)

Now that we have created our lambda function successfully. Let’s write some code for this function

Writing the Lambda Function Code:

import boto3import time
dynamodb = boto3.resource('dynamodb')
table = dynamodb.Table('Users')
def lambda_handler(event, context):
    user_attributes = event['request']['userAttributes']        'userId': user_attributes['sub'],
        'phone_number': user_attributes['phone_number'], 
        'email': user_attributes['email'],
        'given_name': user_attributes['given_name'],
        'family_name': user_attributes['family_name'],
        'birthdate': user_attributes['birthdate'],
        'gender': user_attributes['gender'],
        'createdAt': int(time.time())
    }
    table.put_item(Item=user_item)
    return event  # Required for Cognito to proceed
: Adjust the user attributes based on what you selected when creating your user pool.
Why Return the Event in the Lambda Function?

When Cognito triggers a (such as this one), it expects the function to return the event object unchanged. This is because:

  1. Cognito Relies on the Event Flow
  • Cognito passes an event object to Lambda, which contains user attributes and other metadata.
  • If the function , Cognito assumes the process failed and may interrupt the user sign-up flow.

2. Ensures a Smooth Sign-Up Process

  • Cognito from the Lambda function before continuing with the sign-up.
  • If the response is missing or altered incorrectly, Cognito may reject the sign-up.

3. Required for Post-Confirmation Triggers

  • Since this function is a Post Confirmation Trigger, Cognito expects a return value to proceed with other internal processes (e.g., issuing tokens)

TL; DR: Without returning event, Cognito wouldn’t know if the Lambda function , which could break the user registration process.

Step 5: Setting Up Lambda Permissions

Our Lambda function needs permissions to access DynamoDB:

  1. Head to the Configuration tab of your Lambda function
  1. Click on the role name to open the IAM role
  • Go to Policies in the IAM dashboard
  • Replace the template with this JSON:
{    "Version": "2012-10-17",        {            "Action": [                "dynamodb:GetItem",                "dynamodb:DeleteItem",                "dynamodb:Query"            "Resource": "arn:aws:dynamodb:REGION:ACCOUNT_ID:table/Users"    ]

3. Replace REGION with your AWS region (e.g., us-east-1) and ACCOUNT_ID With your AWS Account ID

4. Name the policy (e.g., “LambdaDynamoDBPolicy”) and save it

5. Attach this policy to your Lambda execution role

Step 6: Connecting Cognito to Lambda

Now we need to trigger our Lambda function when a user signs up:

  • Go to Cognito and navigate to your user pool
  • Under “Extensions,” add a Lambda trigger
  • Select the trigger type as “Sign-up.”
  • Choose the “Post confirmation trigger” option
  • Select the Lambda function we created earlier

test your application with a new user, and ,voila we have our user in our DynamoDB table as you see

Step 7: Setting Up API Gateway

Finally, let’s create an API to interact with our backend:

  • Go to the API Gateway in the AWS console
  • Click “Create API” and select “REST API” (not REST API Private)
  • Choose “New API” and give it a meaningful name (e.g., “ShoeStoreAPI”)
  • For the API Endpoint type, select “Regional”

for the API configurations. Select a new API option and give it a meaningful name (e.g., storeAPI)

You can leave the description blank or write a few sentences about what you’re building.

AWS API Gateway offers , each designed for different use cases:

Understanding API Endpoint Types:

  • Deployed within a specific AWS Region
  • Best for APIs used in the same region or fronted by CloudFront
  • Ideal for internal applications or regional services

:

  • Uses Amazon CloudFront to cache and optimize requests at edge locations
  • Reduces latency for global users
  • Best for public APIs with worldwide users
  • Accessible only within a VPC
  • No direct internet access
  • Ideal for secure, internal APIs within an AWS environment

Step 8: Implementing API Gateway for Shoe Inventory Management

Now that we have our authentication system and database set up, let’s create the actual API endpoints that will allow users to interact with our shoe inventory.

Amazon API Gateway is a fully managed service that makes it easy to create, publish, maintain, and secure APIs at any scale.

in our project, it serves as the for all requests hitting our backend — from creating a new shoe listing to fetching or deleting one.

We’ll use API Gateway to expose that trigger , which in turn interact with our database.

We’ll create a REST API with the following endpoints:

    Each of these endpoints will be connected to a corresponding .

    • On the “Resources” page, click “Create method.”
    • Create each of the following routes:

    After doing these repetitive steps.

    1. Create Lambda Functions for Each Endpoint. For each endpoint, we need to create a corresponding Lambda function. Let’s start with the function to create a new shoe listing:
    • Select “Author from scratch.”
    • Name it “CreateShoeFunction”
    • Select Python 3.13 for the runtime

    Here’s an example code for the functionCreateShoe.

    import boto3import jsonfrom datetime import datetime
    dynamodb = boto3.resource('dynamodb')
    table = dynamodb.Table('Shoes')  # Make sure you've created this table
    def lambda_handler(event, context):
        try:
            # Parse the incoming JSON body
            body = json.loads(event['body'])
            # Check for required fields
            required_fields = ['name', 'brand', 'price', 'size']
            for field in required_fields:
                if field not in body:                    'statusCode': 400,
                        'body': json.dumps(f"Missing required field: {field}")
                    }
            # Create shoe item with unique ID
            shoe_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
            current_time = datetime.now().isoformat()
                'ShoeId': shoe_id,            'brand': body['brand'],            'size': body['size'],
                'description': body.get('description', ''),
                'color': body.get('color', ''),
                'in_stock': body.get('in_stock', True),
                'created_at': current_time,
                'updated_at': current_time
            }        table.put_item(Item=shoe_item)
            # Return success response
            return {            'body': json.dumps({
                    'message': 'Shoe created successfully',
                    'id': shoe_id,            })        # Log the error and return error response
            print(f"Error creating shoe listing: {str(e)}")
            return {            'body': json.dumps(f"Error creating shoe listing: {str(e)}")

    Create similar Lambda functions for the other endpoints (GetAllShoes, GetShoeById, UpdateShoe, DeleteShoe, etc)

    2. Attach Lambda Integrations

    • Return to your API in API Gateway
    • For each route, click “Attach integration.”
    • Choose the corresponding Lambda function for each route:
    • POST /shoes → CreateShoeFunction
    • GET /shoes → GetAllShoesFunction
    • GET /shoes/{id} → GetShoeByIdFunction
    • PUT /shoes/{id} → UpdateShoeFunction
    • DELETE /shoes/{id} → DeleteShoeFunction

    3. Set Up Lambda Permissions. Each Lambda function needs permissions to access the DynamoDB table. Create a policy similar to the one we created earlier, but for the Shoes table:

    {    "Version": "2012-10-17",        {            "Action": [                "dynamodb:GetItem",                "dynamodb:DeleteItem",                "dynamodb:Query"            "Resource": "arn:aws:dynamodb:REGION:ACCOUNT_ID:table/Shoes"    ]

    Attach this policy to each Lambda function’s execution role.

    Creating the DynamoDB Table for Shoes

    Before testing the API, make sure you’ve created a DynamoDB table for the shoe listings (Same steps as the table for users):

    1. Go to the DynamoDB console
    2. Set the table name to “Shoes”
    3. Set the partition key to “ShoeId” with type “String”
    4. Keep the default settings

    Step 8: Testing Your API Endpoints

    Let’s first test our API directly from the AWS console to verify our setup:

    • Navigate to your API in API Gateway
    • Select the endpoint you want to test (e.g., POST /shoes)
    • For POST methods, enter a test payload like:
    {  "name": "Air Jordan 1",  "price": 180,  "color": "red",
      "description": "Classic basketball shoe"
    }
    • Click "Test" and verify you receive a successful response

    Console testing is good for quick verification, but it doesn’t represent the true user experience since it bypasses authentication requirements.

    For a more realistic test, use tools like Postman, Insomnia, or cURL to test your API from outside AWS:

    Create a new shoe listing:

    curl -X POST https://your-api-url/shoes \  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \    "name": "Air Jordan 1",    "price": 180,    "color": "red",
        "description": "Classic basketball shoe"
      }'

    Step 9: Securing Your API

    in a production environment, you’d have to secure your API connection. so that only verified users are allowed to create new listing for example

    Understanding API Testing Contexts

    Before implementing authentication, it’s important to understand the difference between testing environments:

    Why You Needed to Generate a Token (via Cognito) in Thunder Client:

    When you're testing your API , like in Postman, you act like a real user or front-end client. If your API is secured with Cognito User Pools, then:

    Every request must include a (ID Token) in the Authorization header.

    But, When you test directly from the API Gateway console’s “Test” tab, you’re not really going through all the layers because:

    It bypasses Cognito authorizers and IAM checks. and It invokes the Lambda integration if you have the right permissions as the console user (you’re authenticated via AWS already).

    • and checking integration responses — it doesn’t simulate secured access.

    So it works even for endpoints that require Cognito authentication — , not outside it.

    Implementing API Authorization with Cognito

    Follow these steps to secure your API with Amazon Cognito:

    1. Navigate to “Authorizers” from the API Gateway left sidebar
    2. Add a new authorizer and select your previously created user pool
    1. Pay attention to capitalization in “Authorizer” fields
    2. Re-deploy your API after adding the authorizer

    Now if you try the same POST request from an external tool, you’ll receive a 401 Unauthorized error.

    Generating Authentication Tokens

    To make authenticated requests, you’ll need to:

    1. Create a user in your Cognito user pool
    2. Verify their email after signup

    3. Generate an authentication token using the AWS CLI:

    use the command line to run the following command:

    aws cognito-idp initiate-auth --auth-flow USER_PASSWORD_AUTH --client-id 1numv59o72h0nnd8tstbtoe7j8 --auth-parameters USERNAME="40ccd98c-d071-7089-3b8e-c6df11da5656",PASSWORD="StrongPassword#1"

    Replace the client-id, username, and password with your specific values.

    Handling Secret Hash Requirements

    if you get an error that the user pool was configured with a secret hash you’ll need to provide a secret hash. Generate it using this Python script:

    import hmacimport hashlib
    CLIENT_ID = "1numv59o72h0nnd8tstbtoe7j8"
    CLIENT_SECRET = "1u8s45nbbm9ejfqi6l0r2h13iie04s9k7n5unrleb3v2e8tcbvsl"
    USERNAME = "40ccd98c-d071-7089-3b8e-c6df11da5656"
    message = USERNAME + CLIENT_ID
    digest = hmac.new(CLIENT_SECRET.encode(), message.encode(), hashlib.sha256).digest()
    secret_hash = base64.b64encode(digest).decode()
    # Output example: SoqY4XPRbMQ7sKNEglPRLhLQIw1vrY6txxpWs4gx5jc=

    Then modify your authentication command:

    aws cognito-idp initiate-auth --auth-flow USER_PASSWORD_AUTH \  --client-id 1numv59o72h0nnd8tstbtoe7j8 \
      --auth-parameters USERNAME="40ccd98c-d071-7089-3b8e-c6df11da5656",PASSWORD="StrongPassword#1",SECRET_HASH=SoqY4XPRbMQ7sKNEglPRLhLQIw1vrY6txxpWs4gx5jc=copy the ID Token, if you can’t see it in your console make sure to keep hitting enter and go back to your API testing platform ( I use thunder API an extension in VSCODE)

    ⚠️ Important Security Warning ⚠️

    NEVER share your Cognito client ID and Secret in production environments. The credentials shown here are strictly for demonstration purposes. Exposing these credentials in real-world applications would cause a severe security breach.

    Making Authenticated Requests

    After running the authentication command:

    1. Copy the ID Token from the response
    2. Add it to your API testing platform (like Thunder Client in VS Code)
    1. Include an “Authorization” header with the token value
    2. Make your POST request again

    With the proper authentication, you should now successfully create a shoe record securely.

    Congratulations! You’ve now built a complete serverless backend with:

    • API Gateway for request handling
    • Lambda functions for business logic
    • DynamoDB for data storage
    • Cognito for user authentication and API security
    Remember to delete your AWS resources when you’re done with this project to avoid unexpected charges.

    If you’re interested in expanding this project, you now have the understanding and capability to implement additional functions. And if you found the manual AWS console configuration tedious, check out my Terraform guide to learn how Infrastructure as Code can automate these processes much more efficiently. (Coming soon next week)

items.title: Kubernetes v1.33: In-Place Pod Resize Graduated to Beta
items.content:
On behalf of the Kubernetes project, I am excited to announce that the feature (also known as In-Place Pod Vertical Scaling), first introduced as alpha in Kubernetes v1.27, has graduated to and will be enabled by default in the Kubernetes v1.33 release! This marks a significant milestone in making resource management for Kubernetes workloads more flexible and less disruptive.

What is in-place Pod resize?

Traditionally, changing the CPU or memory resources allocated to a container required restarting the Pod. While acceptable for many stateless applications, this could be disruptive for stateful services, batch jobs, or any workloads sensitive to restarts.

In-place Pod resizing allows you to change the CPU and memory requests and limits assigned to containers within a Pod, often without requiring a container restart.

  • The spec.containers[*].resources field in a Pod specification now represents the resources and is mutable for CPU and memory.
  • The status.containerStatuses[*].resources field reflects the resources currently configured on a running container.
  • You can trigger a resize by updating the desired resources in the Pod spec via the new subresource.

You can try it out on a v1.33 Kubernetes cluster by using kubectl to edit a Pod (requires v1.32+):

Why does in-place Pod resize matter?

Kubernetes still excels at scaling workloads horizontally (adding or removing replicas), but in-place Pod resizing unlocks several key benefits for vertical scaling:

  • Stateful applications, long-running batch jobs, and sensitive workloads can have their resources adjusted without suffering the downtime or state loss associated with a Pod restart.
  • Improved Resource Utilization: Scale down over-provisioned Pods without disruption, freeing up resources in the cluster. Conversely, provide more resources to Pods under heavy load without needing a restart.
  • Address transient resource needs more quickly. For example Java applications often need more CPU during startup than during steady-state operation. Start with higher CPU and resize down later.

What's changed between Alpha and Beta?

Since the alpha release in v1.27, significant work has gone into maturing the feature, improving its stability, and refining the user experience based on feedback and further development. Here are the key changes:

Notable user-facing changes

  • Modifying Pod resources must now be done via the Pod's subresource (kubectl patch pod <name> --subresource resize ...). versions v1.32+ support this argument.
  • Resize Status via Conditions: The old field is deprecated. The status of a resize operation is now exposed via two Pod conditions:
    • : Indicates the Kubelet cannot grant the resize immediately (e.g., if temporarily unable, if impossible on the node).
    • : Indicates the resize is accepted and being applied. Errors encountered during this phase are now reported in this condition's message with .

Stability and reliability enhancements

  • Refined Allocated Resources Management: The allocation management logic with the Kubelet was significantly reworked, making it more consistent and robust. The changes eliminated whole classes of bugs, and greatly improved the reliability of in-place Pod resize.
  • Improved Checkpointing & State Tracking: A more robust system for tracking "allocated" and "actuated" resources was implemented, using new checkpoint files (, ) to reliably manage resize state across Kubelet restarts and handle edge cases where runtime-reported resources differ from requested ones. Several bugs related to checkpointing and state restoration were fixed. Checkpointing efficiency was also improved.
  • Enhancements to the Kubelet's Pod Lifecycle Event Generator (PLEG) allow the Kubelet to respond to and complete resizes much more quickly.
  • Enhanced CRI Integration: A new UpdatePodSandboxResources CRI call was added to better inform runtimes and plugins (like NRI) about Pod-level resource changes.
  • Addressed issues related to systemd cgroup drivers, handling of containers without limits, CPU minimum share calculations, container restart backoffs, error propagation, test stability, and more.

Graduating to Beta means the feature is ready for broader adoption, but development doesn't stop here! Here's what the community is focusing on next:

  • Stability and Productionization: Continued focus on hardening the feature, improving performance, and ensuring it is robust for production environments.
  • Working towards relaxing some of the current limitations noted in the documentation, such as allowing memory limit decreases.
  • Work to enable VPA to leverage in-place Pod resize is already underway. A new update mode will allow it to attempt non-disruptive resizes first, or fall back to recreation if needed. This will allow users to benefit from VPA's recommendations with significantly less disruption.
  • Gathering feedback from users adopting the beta feature is crucial for prioritizing further enhancements and addressing any uncovered issues or bugs.

Getting started and providing feedback

With the InPlacePodVerticalScaling feature gate enabled by default in v1.33, you can start experimenting with in-place Pod resizing right away!

As this feature moves through Beta, your feedback is invaluable. Please report any issues or share your experiences via the standard Kubernetes communication channels (GitHub issues, mailing lists, Slack). You can also review the KEP-1287: In-place Update of Pod Resources for the full in-depth design details.

We look forward to seeing how the community leverages in-place Pod resize to build more efficient and resilient applications on Kubernetes!

items.title: Announcing etcd v3.6.0
items.content:
This announcement originally appeared on the etcd blog.

Today, we are releasing etcd v3.6.0, the first minor release since etcd v3.5.0 on June 15, 2021. This release introduces several new features, makes significant progress on long-standing efforts like downgrade support and migration to v3store, and addresses numerous critical & major issues. It also includes major optimizations in memory usage, improving efficiency and performance.

In addition to the features of v3.6.0, etcd has joined Kubernetes as a SIG (sig-etcd), enabling us to improve project sustainability. We've introduced systematic robustness testing to ensure correctness and reliability. Through the etcd-operator Working Group, we plan to improve usability as well.

What follows are the most significant changes introduced in etcd v3.6.0, along with the discussion of the roadmap for future development. For a detailed list of changes, please refer to the CHANGELOG-3.6.

A heartfelt thank you to all the contributors who made this release possible!

etcd takes security seriously. To enhance software security in v3.6.0, we have improved our workflow checks by integrating to scan the source code and to scan container images. These improvements have also been backported to supported stable releases.

The v2store has been deprecated since etcd v3.4 but could still be enabled via . It remained the source of truth for membership data. In etcd v3.6.0, v2store can no longer be enabled as the flag has been removed, and v3store has become the sole source of truth for membership data.

While v2store still exists in v3.6.0, etcd will fail to start if it contains any data other than membership information. To assist with migration, etcd v3.5.18+ provides the command, which verifies that v2store contains only membership data (see PR 19113).

Compared to v2store, v3store offers better performance and transactional support. It is also the actively maintained storage engine moving forward.

The removal of v2store is still ongoing and is tracked in issues/12913.

etcd v3.6.0 is the first version to fully support downgrade. The effort for this downgrade task spans both versions 3.5 and 3.6, and all related work is tracked in issues/11716.

At a high level, the process involves migrating the data schema to the target version (e.g., v3.5), followed by a rolling downgrade.

Ensure the cluster is healthy, and take a snapshot backup. Validate whether the downgrade is valid:

If the downgrade is valid, enable downgrade mode:

etcd will then migrate the data schema in the background. Once complete, proceed with the rolling downgrade.

In etcd v3.6.0, we introduced Kubernetes-style feature gates for managing new features. Previously, we indicated unstable features through the prefix in feature flag names. The prefix was removed once the feature was stable, causing a breaking change. Now, features will start in Alpha, progress to Beta, then GA, or get deprecated. This ensures a much smoother upgrade and downgrade experience for users.

etcd now supports and endpoints, aligning with Kubernetes' Liveness and Readiness probes. indicates whether the etcd instance is alive, while indicates when it is ready to serve requests. This feature has also been backported to release-3.5 (starting from v3.5.11) and release-3.4 (starting from v3.4.29). See livez/readyz for details.

The existing endpoint remains functional. is similar to /health?serializable=true, while is similar to or /health?serializable=false. Clearly, the and endpoints provide clearer semantics and are easier to understand.

In etcd v3.6.0, the new discovery protocol v3discovery was introduced, based on clientv3. It facilitates the discovery of all cluster members during the bootstrap phase.

The previous v2discovery protocol, based on clientv2, has been deprecated. Additionally, the public discovery service at https://discovery.etcd.io/, which relied on v2discovery, is no longer maintained.

In this release, we reduced average memory consumption by at least 50% (see Figure 1). This improvement is primarily due to two changes:

  • The default value of has been reduced from 100,000 in v3.5 to 10,000 in v3.6. As a result, etcd v3.6 now retains only about 10% of the history records compared to v3.5.
  • Raft history is compacted more frequently, as introduced in PR/18825.

Memory usage comparison between etcd v3.5.20 and v3.6.0-rc.2 under different read/write ratios. Each subplot shows the memory usage over time with a specific read/write ratio. The red line represents etcd v3.5.20, while the teal line represents v3.6.0-rc.2. Across all tested ratios, v3.6.0-rc.2 exhibits lower and more stable memory usage.

Compared to v3.5, etcd v3.6 delivers an average performance improvement of approximately 10% in both read and write throughput (see Figure 2, 3, 4 and 5). This improvement is not attributed to any single major change, but rather the cumulative effect of multiple minor enhancements. One such example is the optimization of the free page queries introduced in PR/419.

Read throughput comparison between etcd v3.5.20 and v3.6.0-rc.2 under a high write ratio. The read/write ratio is 0.0078, meaning 1 read per 128 writes. The right bar shows the percentage improvement in read throughput of v3.6.0-rc.2 over v3.5.20, ranging from 3.21% to 25.59%.

Read throughput comparison between etcd v3.5.20 and v3.6.0-rc.2 under a high read ratio. The read/write ratio is 8, meaning 8 reads per write. The right bar shows the percentage improvement in read throughput of v3.6.0-rc.2 over v3.5.20, ranging from 4.38% to 27.20%.

Write throughput comparison between etcd v3.5.20 and v3.6.0-rc.2 under a high write ratio. The read/write ratio is 0.0078, meaning 1 read per 128 writes. The right bar shows the percentage improvement in write throughput of v3.6.0-rc.2 over v3.5.20, ranging from 2.95% to 24.24%.

Write throughput comparison between etcd v3.5.20 and v3.6.0-rc.2 under a high read ratio. The read/write ratio is 8, meaning 8 reads per write. The right bar shows the percentage improvement in write throughput of v3.6.0-rc.2 over v3.5.20, ranging from 3.86% to 28.37%.

Old binaries are incompatible with new schema versions

Old etcd binaries are not compatible with newer data schema versions. For example, etcd 3.5 cannot start with data created by etcd 3.6, and etcd 3.4 cannot start with data created by either 3.5 or 3.6.

When downgrading etcd, it's important to follow the documented downgrade procedure. Simply replacing the binary or image will result in the incompatibility issue.

Peer endpoints no longer serve client requests

Client endpoints () are intended to serve client requests only, while peer endpoints (--initial-advertise-peer-urls) are intended solely for peer communication. However, due to an implementation oversight, the peer endpoints were also able to handle client requests in etcd 3.4 and 3.5. This behavior was misleading and encouraged incorrect usage patterns. In etcd 3.6, this misleading behavior was corrected via PR/13565; peer endpoints no longer serve client requests.

Clear boundary between etcdctl and etcdutl

Both and are command line tools. is an offline utility designed to operate directly on etcd data files, while is an online tool that interacts with etcd over a network. Previously, there were some overlapping functionalities between the two, but these overlaps were removed in 3.6.0.

  • Removed etcdctl defrag --data-dir

    The command only support online defragmentation and no longer supports offline defragmentation. To perform offline defragmentation, use the etcdutl defrag --data-dir command instead.

  • Removed

    no longer supports retrieving the status of a snapshot. Use the command instead.

  • Removed

    no longer supports restoring from a snapshot. Use the command instead.

Correctness has always been a top priority for the etcd project. In the process of developing 3.6.0, we found and fixed a few notable bugs that could lead to data inconsistency in specific cases. These fixes have been backported to previous releases, but we believe they deserve special mention here.

  • Data Inconsistency when Crashing Under Load

Previously, when etcd was applying data, it would update the consistent-index first, followed by committing the data. However, these operations were not atomic. If etcd crashed in between, it could lead to data inconsistency (see issue/13766). The issue was introduced in v3.5.0, and fixed in v3.5.3 with PR/13854.

  • Durability API guarantee broken in single node cluster

When a client writes data and receives a success response, the data is expected to be persisted. However, the data might be lost if etcd crashes immediately after sending the success response to the client. This was a legacy issue (see issue/14370) affecting all previous releases. It was addressed in v3.4.21 and v3.5.5 with PR/14400, and fixed in raft side in main branch (now release-3.6) with PR/14413.

  • Revision Inconsistency when Crashing During Defragmentation

If etcd crashed during the defragmentation operation, upon restart, it might reapply some entries which had already been applied, accordingly leading to the revision inconsistency issue (see the discussions in PR/14685). The issue was introduced in v3.5.0, and fixed in v3.5.6 with PR/14730.

The issue was introduced in etcd v3.5.1, and resolved in v3.5.20.

: users are required to first upgrade to etcd v3.5.20 (or a higher patch version) before upgrading to etcd v3.6.0; otherwise, the upgrade may fail.

We introduced the Robustness testing to verify correctness, which has always been our top priority. It plays traffic of various types and volumes against an etcd cluster, concurrently injects a random failpoint, records all operations (including both requests and responses), and finally performs a linearizability check. It also verifies that the Watch APIs guarantees have not been violated. The robustness test increases our confidence in ensuring the quality of each etcd release.

We have migrated most of the etcd workflow tests to Kubernetes' Prow testing infrastructure to take advantage of its benefit, such as nice dashboards for viewing test results and the ability for contributors to rerun failed tests themselves.

While retaining all existing supported platforms, we have promoted Linux/ARM64 to Tier 1 support. For more details, please refer to issues/15951. For the complete list of supported platforms, see supported-platform.

We have published an official guide on how to bump dependencies for etcd’s main branch and stable releases. It also covers how to update the Go version. For more details, please refer to dependency_management. With this guide available, any contributors can now help with dependency upgrades.

bbolt and raft are two core dependencies of etcd.

Both etcd v3.4 and v3.5 depend on bbolt v1.3, while etcd v3.6 depends on bbolt v1.4.

For the release-3.4 and release-3.5 branches, raft is included in the etcd repository itself, so etcd v3.4 and v3.5 do not depend on an external raft module. Starting from etcd v3.6, raft was moved to a separate repository (raft), and the first standalone raft release is v3.6.0. As a result, etcd v3.6.0 depends on raft v3.6.0.

Please see the table below for a summary:

We upgraded grpc-gateway from v1 to v2 via PR/16595 in etcd v3.6.0. This is a major step toward migrating to protobuf-go, the second major version of the Go protocol buffer API implementation.

grpc-gateway@v2 is designed to work with protobuf-go. However, etcd v3.6 still depends on the deprecated gogo/protobuf, which is actually protocol buffer v1 implementation. To resolve this incompatibility, we applied a patch to the generated *.pb.gw.go files to convert v1 messages to v2 messages.

grpc-ecosystem/go-grpc-middleware/providers/prometheus

There are exciting developments in the etcd community that reflect our ongoing commitment to strengthening collaboration, improving maintainability, and evolving the project’s governance.

etcd has officially become a Kubernetes Special Interest Group: SIG-etcd. This change reflects etcd’s critical role as the primary datastore for Kubernetes and establishes a more structured and transparent home for long-term stewardship and cross-project collaboration. The new SIG designation will help streamline decision-making, align roadmaps with Kubernetes needs, and attract broader community involvement.

New contributors, maintainers, and reviewers

We’ve seen increasing engagement from contributors, which has resulted in the addition of three new maintainers:

Their continued contributions have been instrumental in driving the project forward.

We also welcome two new reviewers to the project:

We appreciate their dedication to code quality and their willingness to take on broader review responsibilities within the community.

We've formed a new release team led by ivanvc and jmhbnz, streamlining the release process by automating many previously manual steps. Inspired by Kubernetes SIG Release, we've adopted several best practices, including clearly defined release team roles and the introduction of release shadows to support knowledge sharing and team sustainability. These changes have made our releases smoother and more reliable, allowing us to approach each release with greater confidence and consistency.

Introducing the etcd Operator Working Group

To further advance etcd’s operational excellence, we have formed a new working group: WG-etcd-operator. The working group is dedicated to enabling the automatic and efficient operation of etcd clusters that run in the Kubernetes environment using an etcd-operator.

The legacy v2store has been deprecated since etcd v3.4, and the flag was removed entirely in v3.6. This means that starting from v3.6, there is no longer a way to enable or use the v2store. However, etcd still bootstraps internally from the legacy v2 snapshots. To address this inconsistency, We plan to change etcd to bootstrap from the v3store and replay the WAL entries based on the . The work is being tracked in issues/12913.

One of the most persistent challenges remains the large range of queries from the kube-apiserver, which can lead to process crashes due to their unpredictable nature. The range stream feature, originally outlined in the v3.5 release blog/Future roadmaps, remains an idea worth revisiting to address the challenges of large range queries.

For more details and upcoming plans, please refer to the etcd roadmap.

items.title: Docker at Microsoft Build 2025: Where Secure Software Meets Intelligent Innovation
items.author: Melissa Sussmann
items.content:
This year at Microsoft Build, Docker will blend developer experience, security, and AI innovation with our latest product announcements. Whether you attend in person at the Seattle Convention Center or tune in online, you’ll see how Docker is redefining the way teams build, secure, and scale modern applications.

Docker’s Vision for Developers

At Microsoft Build 2025, Docker’s EVP of Product and Engineering, Tushar Jain, will present the company’s vision for AI-native software delivery, prioritizing simplicity, security, and developer flow. His session will explore how Docker is helping teams adopt AI without complexity and scale confidently from local development to production using the workflows they already trust.

This vision starts with security. Today’s developers are expected to manage a growing number of vulnerabilities, stay compliant with evolving standards, and still ship software on time. Docker helps teams simplify container security by integrating with tools like Microsoft Defender, Azure Container Registry, and AKS. This makes it easier to build secure, production-ready applications without overhauling existing workflows.

This session explores how Docker is streamlining agentic AI development by bringing models and MCP tools together in one familiar environment. Learn how to build agentic AI with your existing workflows and commands. Explore curated AI tools on Docker Hub to get inspired and jumpstart your projects. No steep learning curve is required! With built-in security, access control, and secret management, Docker handles the heavy lifting so you can focus on building smarter, more capable agents.

Don’t miss our follow-up demo session with Principal Engineer Jim Clark. He’ll show how to build an agentic app that uses Docker’s latest AI tools and familiar workflows.

Visit Docker at Booth #400 to see us in action

Throughout the conference, Docker will be live at . Drop by for demos, expert walkthroughs, and to try out Docker Hardened Images, Model Runner, and MCP Catalog and Toolkit. Our product, engineering, and DevRel teams will be on-site to answer questions and help you get hands-on.

Party with your fellow Developers at MOPOP

We’re hosting an evening event at one of Seattle’s most iconic pop culture venues to celebrate the launch of our latest tools.

Monday, May 19 7:00–10:00 PM Museum of Pop Culture, Seattle

Enjoy live demos, food and drinks, access to Docker engineers and leaders, and private after-hours access to the museum. Space is limited. RSVP now to reserve your spot!

items.title: Continue to take control over your code with Amazon Q Developer’s new context features
items.author: Eva Knight
items.content:
Since then, Amazon Q Developer released additional features in VS Code to help provide users with more control over their conversations and enhance their ability to maintain development context across longer periods. These new capabilities make your interactions with Amazon Q Developer not just more efficient, but also more contextual and persistent—helping you maintain your development flow, even when work spans hours or days. Now, let’s jump in and explore some of the new features available today.

We’ve all been there—you’re deep in conversation with Amazon Q Developer, maybe you’re debugging an authentication problem, optimizing a complex database query, or designing a new API structure. You and Amazon Q Developer have been going back and forth, uncovering insights and piecing together solutions.

Then reality intervenes. You close your IDE to focus on another task, step away for a meeting, or maybe update your computer. When you finally return to your IDE, ready to dive back in, you’re met with a blank chat window. All that context, all those valuable exchanges—gone. You find yourself trying to reconstruct your train of thought, wasting precious time and momentum.

Amazon Q Developer now preserves your conversations across your IDE sessions. Instead of starting from scratch each time you open the IDE, you can now come back to your conversation and pick up right where you left off.

Conversation History Search

It isn’t just after a closed IDE session or coming back to your computer after a long-weekend that you want Amazon Q Developer to remember what you have been working on. Sometimes you need to reference a previous solution — maybe Amazon Q Developer gave you some good advice on optimizing your database queries that you want to use elsewhere, or maybe you decided to work on some front-end components so you could have fresh eyes for the API performance issue you’ve been working at.

Now, you can access your previous conversations with Amazon Q Developer by clicking on the search icon in the top right corner of your chat window. You can quickly locate specific discussions by typing keywords into the search bar, then either review the previous exchange or continue the conversation where you left off.

Fig 1 – View chat history feature in the Amazon Q Developer VS Code chat interface.

But what if you need to share these insights with a teammate or want to keep a local record for future reference? You can now easily export your chat sessions as markdown files, preserving all the valuable information for offline use or collaboration. To do this, click the export button located directly to the right of the chat history button. Alternatively, when browsing your chat history, you can export individual sessions by clicking the three dots on the right side of each conversation entry.

Fig 2 – Export chat feature in the Amazon Q Developer VS Code chat interface.

Increasing your control over context

Last July, we announced the ability to use @workspace in your chat session to provide comprehensive context across your entire application within the IDE. To provide more control over what Amazon Q Developer uses as context, earlier this year we released the ability to use the @ symbol in the chat to include specific folders or files as context for your conversation.

We are now taking your level of control one step further to allow you to use @ in your conversation to find and include classes, functions, and global variables into the input context. Rather than leaving it up to Amazon Q to determine the relevant files, folders, or functions for your request, you can continue to be more explicit with your request to receive the most relevant and accurate responses.

Fig 3 – Context selection in Amazon Q Developer chat, using “@” to show relevant folders, files, functions.

Amazon Q Developer is continuing to evolve its features that help put developers in control of their coding experience. By offering conversation persistence, history search, and export capabilities, Amazon Q Developer works to create continuity that allows developers to maintain their momentum across sessions and easily revisit past solutions. The expanded context control features empower developers to fine-tune their interactions with Amazon Q Developer, and receive more precise and relevant responses.

To get started with these features in VS Code, visit the Amazon Q Developer Getting Started guide and explore the full range of capabilities that can help you create impressive software more efficiently.

items.title: New Amazon EC2 P6-B200 instances powered by NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs to accelerate AI innovations

items.content:
Amazon EC2 P6-B200 instances accelerate a broad range of GPU-enabled workloads but are especially well-suited for large-scale distributed AI training and inferencing for foundation models (FMs) with reinforcement learning (RL) and distillation, multimodal training and inference, and HPC applications such as climate modeling, drug discovery, seismic analysis, and insurance risk modeling.

When combined with Elastic Fabric Adapter (EFAv4) networking, hyperscale clustering by EC2 UltraClusters, and advanced virtualization and security capabilities by AWS Nitro System, you can train and serve FMs with increased speed, scale, and security. These instances also deliver up to two times the performance for AI training (time to train) and inference (tokens/sec) compared to EC2 P5en instances.

You can accelerate time-to-market for training FMs and deliver faster inference throughput, which lowers inference cost and helps increase adoption of generative AI applications as well as increased processing performance for HPC applications.

Here are the specs for EC2 P6-B200 instances:

These instances feature up to 125 percent improvement in GPU TFLOPs, 27 percent increase in GPU memory size, and 60 percent increase in GPU memory bandwidth compared to P5en instances.

When launching P6-B200 instances, you can use AWS Deep Learning AMIs (DLAMI) to support EC2 P6-B200 instances. DLAMI provides ML practitioners and researchers with the infrastructure and tools to quickly build scalable, secure, distributed ML applications in preconfigured environments.

Amazon EC2 P6-B200 instances are available today in the US West (Oregon) Region and can be purchased as EC2 Capacity blocks for ML.
