items.title: Accelerate CI/CD pipelines with the new AWS CodeBuild Docker Server capability
items.author: Donnie Prakoso
items.content:
Starting today, you can use AWS CodeBuild Docker Server capability to provision a dedicated and persistent Docker server directly within your CodeBuild project. With Docker Server capability, you can accelerate your Docker image builds by centralizing image building to a remote host, which reduces wait times and increases overall efficiency.

From my benchmark, with this Docker Server capability, I reduced the total building time by 98 percent, from 24 minutes and 54 seconds to 16 seconds. Here’s a quick look at this feature from my AWS CodeBuild projects.

AWS CodeBuild is a fully managed continuous integration service that compiles source code, runs tests, and produces software packages ready for deployment. Building Docker images is one of the most common use cases for CodeBuild customers, and the service has progressively improved this experience over time by releasing features such as Docker layer caching and reserved capacity features to improve Docker build performance.

With the new Docker Server capability, you can reduce build time for your applications by providing a persistent Docker server with consistent caching. When enabled in a CodeBuild project, a dedicated Docker server is provisioned with persistent storage that maintains your Docker layer cache. This server can handle multiple concurrent Docker build operations, with all builds benefiting from the same centralized cache.

Let me walk you through a demonstration that showcases the benefits with the new Docker Server capability.

For this demonstration, I’m building a complex, multi-layered Docker image based on the official AWS CodeBuild curated Docker images repository, specifically the Dockerfile for building a standard Ubuntu image. This image contains numerous dependencies and tools required for modern continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines, making it a good example of the type of large Docker builds that development teams regularly perform.


# Copyright 2020-2024 Amazon.com, Inc. or its affiliates. All Rights Reserved.
#
# Licensed under the Amazon Software License (the "License"). You may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# A copy of the License is located at
#
#    http://aws.amazon.com/asl/
#
# or in the "license" file accompanying this file.
# This file is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS, WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and limitations under the License.
FROM public.ecr.aws/ubuntu/ubuntu:20.04 AS core

ARG DEBIAN_FRONTEND="noninteractive"

# Install git, SSH, Git, Firefox, GeckoDriver, Chrome, ChromeDriver,  stunnel, AWS Tools, configure SSM, AWS CLI v2, env tools for runtimes: Dotnet, NodeJS, Ruby, Python, PHP, Java, Go, .NET, Powershell Core,  Docker, Composer, and other utilities
COMMAND REDACTED FOR BREVITY
# Activate runtime versions specific to image version.
RUN n $NODE_14_VERSION
RUN pyenv  global $PYTHON_39_VERSION
RUN phpenv global $PHP_80_VERSION
RUN rbenv  global $RUBY_27_VERSION
RUN goenv global  $GOLANG_15_VERSION

# Configure SSH
COPY ssh_config /root/.ssh/config
COPY runtimes.yml /codebuild/image/config/runtimes.yml
COPY dockerd-entrypoint.sh /usr/local/bin/dockerd-entrypoint.sh
COPY legal/bill_of_material.txt /usr/share/doc/bill_of_material.txt
COPY amazon-ssm-agent.json /etc/amazon/ssm/amazon-ssm-agent.json

ENTRYPOINT ["/usr/local/bin/dockerd-entrypoint.sh"]

This Dockerfile creates a comprehensive build environment with multiple programming languages, build tools, and dependencies – exactly the type of image that would benefit from persistent caching.

In the build specification (buildspec), I use the command:

version: 0.2
phases:
  build:
    commands:
      - cd ubuntu/standard/5.0
      - docker buildx build -t codebuild-ubuntu:latest .

To enable the Docker Server capability, I navigate to the AWS CodeBuild console and select . I can also enable this capability when editing existing CodeBuild projects.

I fill in all details and configuration. In the section, I select .

Then, I scroll down and find Docker server configuration and select Enable docker server for this project. When I select this option, I can choose a compute type configuration for the Docker server. When I’m finished with the configurations, I create this project.

Now, let’s see the Docker Server capability in action.

The initial build takes approximately 24 minutes and 54 seconds to complete because it needs to download and compile all dependencies from scratch. This is expected for the first build of such a complex image.

For subsequent builds with no code changes, the build takes only 16 seconds and that shows 98% reduction in build time.

Looking at the logs, I can see that with Docker Server, most layers are pulled from the persistent cache:

The persistent caching provided by the Docker Server maintains all layers between builds, which is particularly valuable for large, complex Docker images with many layers. This demonstrates how Docker Server can dramatically improve throughput for teams running numerous Docker builds in their CI/CD pipelines.

Here are a couple of things to note:

  • – The feature is available for both x86 (Linux) and ARM builds.
  • – To learn more about pricing for Docker Server capability, refer to the AWS CodeBuild pricing page.
  • – This feature is available in all AWS Regions where AWS CodeBuild is offered. For more information about the AWS Regions where CodeBuild is available, see the AWS Regions page.

items.title: Kubernetes 1.33: Job's SuccessPolicy Goes GA
items.content:
On behalf of the Kubernetes project, I'm pleased to announce that Job has graduated to General Availability (GA) as part of the v1.33 release.

About Job's Success Policy

In batch workloads, you might want to use leader-follower patterns like MPI, in which the leader controls the execution, including the followers' lifecycle.

In this case, you might want to mark it as succeeded even if some of the indexes failed. Unfortunately, a leader-follower Kubernetes Job that didn't use a success policy, in most cases, would have to require Pods to finish successfully for that Job to reach an overall succeeded state.

For Kubernetes Jobs, the API allows you to specify the early exit criteria using the field (you can only use the field for an indexed Job). Which describes a set of rules either using a list of succeeded indexes for a job, or defining a minimal required size of succeeded indexes.

This newly stable field is especially valuable for scientific simulation, AI/ML and High-Performance Computing (HPC) batch workloads. Users in these areas often run numerous experiments and may only need a specific number to complete successfully, rather than requiring all of them to succeed. In this case, the leader index failure is the only relevant Job exit criteria, and the outcomes for individual follower Pods are handled only indirectly via the status of the leader index. Moreover, followers do not know when they can terminate themselves.

After Job meets any , the Job is marked as succeeded, and all Pods are terminated including the running ones.

The following excerpt from a Job manifest, using .successPolicy.rules[0].succeededCount, shows an example of using a custom success policy:

Here, the Job is marked as succeeded when one index succeeded regardless of its number. Additionally, you can constrain index numbers against in .successPolicy.rules[0].succeededCount as shown below:

This example shows that the Job will be marked as succeeded once a Pod with a specific index (Pod index 0) has succeeded.

Once the Job either reaches one of the rules, or achieves its criteria based on , the Job controller within kube-controller-manager adds the condition to the Job status. After that, the job-controller initiates cleanup and termination of Pods for Jobs with condition. Eventually, Jobs obtain condition when the job-controller finished cleanup and termination.

If you are interested in working on new features in the space I recommend subscribing to our Slack channel and attending the regular community meetings.

items.title: Accelerate the modernization of Mainframe and VMware workloads with AWS Transform
items.author: Matheus Guimaraes
items.content:
Generative AI has brought many new possibilities to organizations. It has equipped them with new abilities to retire technical debt, modernize legacy systems, and build agile infrastructure to help unlock the value that is trapped in their internal data. However, many enterprises still rely heavily on legacy IT infrastructure, particularly mainframes and VMware-based systems. These platforms have been the backbone of critical operations for decades, but they hinder organizations’ ability to innovate, scale effectively, and reduce technical debt in an era where cloud-first strategies dominate. The need to modernize these workloads is clear, but the journey has traditionally been complex and risky.

The complexity spans multiple dimensions. Financially, organizations face mounting licensing costs and expensive migration projects. Technically, they must untangle legacy dependencies while meeting compliance requirements. Organizationally, they must manage the transition of teams who’ve built careers around legacy systems and navigate undocumented institutional knowledge.

AWS Transform directly addresses these challenges with purpose-built agentic AI that accelerates and de-risks your legacy modernization. It automates the assessment, planning, and transformation of both mainframe and VMware workloads into cloud based architectures, streamlining the entire process. Through intelligent insights, automated code transformation, and human-in-the-loop workflows, organizations can now tackle even the most challenging modernization projects with greater confidence and efficiency.

AWS Transform for mainframe is the first agentic AI service for modernizing mainframe workloads at scale. The specialized mainframe agent accelerates mainframe modernization by automating complex, resource-intensive tasks across every phase of modernization — from initial assessment to final deployment. It streamlines the migration of legacy applications built on IBM z/OS, including COBOL, CICS, DB2, and VSAM, to modern cloud environments–cutting modernization timelines from years to months.

Let’s look at a few examples of how AWS Transform can help you through different aspects of the migration process.

– AWS Transform provides comprehensive insights into your codebase, automatically examining mainframe codebases, creating detailed dependency graphs, measuring code complexity, and identifying component relationships

– AWS Transform for mainframe creates comprehensive technical and functional documentation of mainframe applications, preserving critical knowledge about features, program logic, and data flows. You can interact with the generated documentation through an AI-powered chat interface to discover and retrieve information quickly.

Business rule logic extraction – AWS Transform extracts and presents complex logic in plain language so you can gain visibility into business processes embedded within legacy applications. This enables both business and technical stakeholders to gain a greater understanding of application functionality.

– AWS Transform offers sophisticated code decomposition tools, including interactive dependency graphs and domain separation capabilities, enabling users to visualize and modify relationships between components while identifying key business functions. The solution also streamlines migration planning through an interactive wave sequence planner that considers user preferences to generate optimized migration strategies.

Modernization Wave planning – With its specialized agent, AWS Transform for mainframe creates prioritized modernization wave sequences based on code and data dependencies, code volume, and business priorities. It enables modernization teams to make data-driven, customized migration plans that align to their specific organizational needs.

– AWS Transform can refactor millions of lines of mainframe code in minutes, converting COBOL, VSAM, and DB2 systems into modern Java Spring Boot applications while maintaining functional equivalence and transforming CICS transactions into web services and JCL batch processes into Groovy scripts. The solution provides high-quality output through configurable settings and bundled runtime capabilities, producing Java code that emphasizes readability, maintainability, and technical excellence.

– AWS Transform provides customizable deployment templates that streamline the deployment process through user-defined inputs. For added efficiency, the solution bundles the selected runtime version with the migrated application, enabling seamless deployment as a complete package.

By integrating intelligent documentation analysis, business logic extraction, and human-in-the-loop collaboration capabilities, AWS Transform helps organizations accelerate their mainframe transformation while reducing risk and maintaining business continuity.

With rapid changes in VMware licensing and support model, organizations are increasingly exploring alternatives despite the difficulties associated with migrating and modernizing VMware workloads. This is aggravated by the fact that the accumulation of technical debt typically creates complex, poorly documented environments managed by multiple teams, leading to vendor lock-in and collaboration challenges that hinder migration efforts further.

AWS Transform is the first agentic AI service for VMware modernization of its kind that helps you to overcome those difficulties. It can offset risk and accelerate the modernization of VMware workloads by automating application discovery, dependency mapping, migration planning, network conversion, and EC2 instance optimization, reducing manual effort and accelerating cloud adoption.

The process is organized into four phases: inventory discovery, wave planning, network conversion, and server migration. It uses agentic AI capabilities to analyze and map complex VMware environments, converting network configurations into AWS built-in constructs and helps you to orchestrate dependency-aware migration waves for seamless cutovers. In addition, it also provides a collaborative web interface that keeps AWS teams, partners, and customers aligned throughout the modernization journey.

Let’s take a quick tour to see how this works.

Before you can start using the service, you must first enable it by navigating to the AWS Transform console. AWS Transform requires AWS IAM Identity Center (IdC) to manage users and setup appropriate permissions. If you don’t yet have IdC set up it will ask you to configure it first and return to the AWS Transform console later to continue the process.

With IdC available, you can then proceed to choosing the encryption settings. AWS Transform gives you the option to use a default AWS managed key or you can use your own custom keys through AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS).

After completing this step, AWS Transform will be enabled. You can manage admin access to the console by navigating to and using the search box to find them. You must create users or groups in IdC first if they don’t already exist. The service console will help admins provision users who will get access to the web app. Each provisioned user receives an email with a link to set password and get their personalized URL for the webapp.

You interact with AWS Transform through a dedicated web experience. To get the url, navigate to where you can check your configurations and copy the links to the AWS Transform web experience where you and your teams can start using the service.

AWS Transform can discover your VMware environment either automatically through AWS Application Discovery Service collectors or you can provide your own data by importing existing RVTools export files.

To get started, choose the Create or select connectors task and provide the account IDs for one or more AWS accounts that will be used for discovery. This will generate links that you can follow to authorize each account for usage within AWS Transform. You can then move on to the task, where you can choose to install AWS Application Discovery Service collectors or upload your own files such as exports from RVTools.

The steps for the provisioning phase are similar to the ones described earlier for discovery. You connect target AWS accounts by entering their account IDs and validating the authorization requests which will then enable the next steps such as the Generate VPC configuration step. Here, you can import your RVTools files or NSX exports from Import/Export from NSX, if applicable, and enable AWS Transform to understand your networking requirements.

Review the proposed changes and, if you’re happy, start the deployment process of the AWS resources to the target accounts.

AWS Transform requires you to set up AWS Application Migration Service (MGN) in the target AWS accounts to automate the migration process. Choose the task and use the link to navigate to the service console, then follow the instructions to configure it.

After setting up service permissions, you’ll proceed to the implementation phase of the waves created by AWS Transform and start the migration process. For each wave, you’ll first be asked to make various choices such as setting the sizing preference and tenancy for the Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2) instances. Confirm your selections and continue following the instructions given by AWS Transform until you reach the Deploy replication agents stage, where you can start the migration for that wave.

After you start the waves migration process, you can switch to the dashboard at any time to check on progress.

With its agentic AI capabilities, AWS Transform offers a powerful solution for accelerating and de-risking mainframe and VMware modernization workloads. By automating complex assessment and transformation processes, AWS Transform reduces the time associated with legacy system migration while minimizing the potential for errors and business disruption enabling more agile, efficient, and future-ready IT environments within your organization.

–  AWS Transform for mainframe is available in US East (N. Virginia) and Europe (Frankfurt) Regions. AWS Transform for VMware offers different availability options for data collection and migrations. Please refer to the AWS Transform for VMware FAQ for more details.

–  Currently, we offer our core features—including assessment and transformation—at no cost to AWS customers.

Here are a few links for further reading.

items.title: AWS Transform for .NET, the first agentic AI service for modernizing .NET applications at scale
items.author: Prasad Rao
items.content:
I started my career as a .NET developer and have seen .NET evolve over the last couple of decades. Like many of you, I also developed multiple enterprise applications in .NET Framework that ran only on Windows. I fondly remember building my first enterprise application with .NET Framework. Although it served us well, the technology landscape has significantly shifted. Now that there is an open source and cross-platform version of .NET that can run on Linux, these legacy enterprise applications built on .NET Framework need to be ported and modernized.

The benefits of porting to Linux are compelling: applications cost 40 percent less to operate because they save on Windows licensing costs, run 1.5–2 times faster with improved performance, and handle growing workloads with 50 percent better scalability. Having helped port several applications, I can say the effort is worth the rewards.

However, porting .NET Framework applications to cross-platform .NET is a labor-intensive and error-prone process. You have to perform multiple steps, such as analyzing the codebase, detecting incompatibilities, implementing fixes while porting the code, and then validating the changes. For enterprises, the challenge becomes even more complex because they might have hundreds of .NET Framework applications in their portfolio.

Now that we’ve incorporated your valuable feedback and suggestions, we’re excited to announce today the general availability of AWS Transform for .NET. We’ve also added new capabilities to support projects with private NuGet packages, port model-view-controller (MVC) Razor views to ASP .NET Core Razor views, and execute the ported unit tests.

I’ll expand on the key new capabilities in a moment, but let’s first take a quick look at the two porting experiences of AWS Transform for .NET.

Enterprise digital transformation is typically driven by central teams responsible for modernizing hundreds of applications across multiple business units. Different teams have ownership of different applications and their respective repositories. Success requires close coordination between these teams and the application owners and developers across business units. To accelerate this modernization at scale, AWS Transform for .NET provides a web experience that enables teams to connect directly to source code repositories and efficiently transform multiple applications across the organization. For select applications requiring dedicated developer attention, the same agent capabilities are available to developers as an extension for Visual Studio IDE.

Let’s start by looking at how the web experience of AWS Transform for .NET helps port hundreds of .NET applications at scale.

To get started with the web experience of AWS Transform, I onboard using the steps outlined in the documentation, sign in using my credentials, and create a job for .NET modernization.

AWS Transform for .NET creates a job plan, which is a sequence of steps that the agent will execute to assess, discover, analyze, and transform applications at scale. It then waits for me to set up a connector to connect to my source code repositories.

After the connector is in place, AWS Transform begins discovering repositories in my account. It conducts an assessment focused on three key areas: repository dependencies, required private packages and third-party libraries, and supported project types within your repositories.

Based on this assessment, it generates a recommended transformation plan. The plan orders repositories according to their last modification dates, dependency relationships, private package requirements, and the presence of supported project types.

AWS Transform for .NET then prepares for the transformation process by requesting specific inputs, such as the target branch destination, target .NET version, and the repositories to be transformed.

To select the repositories to transform, I have two options: use the recommended plan or customize the transformation plan by selecting repositories manually. For selecting repositories manually, I can use the UI or download the repository mapping and upload the customized list.

AWS Transform for .NET automatically ports the application code, builds the ported code, executes unit tests, and commits the ported code to a new branch in my repository. It provides a comprehensive transformation summary, including modified files, test outcomes, and suggested fixes for any remaining work.

While the web experience helps accelerate large-scale porting, some applications may require developer attention. For these cases, the same agent capabilities are available in the Visual Studio IDE.

Now, let’s explore how AWS Transform for .NET works within Visual Studio.

I open a .NET Framework solution, and in the , I see the context menu item Port project with AWS Transform for an individual project.

I provide the required inputs, such as the target .NET version and the approval for the agents to autonomously transform code, execute unit tests, generate a transformation summary, and validate Linux-readiness.

I can review the code changes made by the agents locally and continue updating my codebase.

Let’s now explore some of the key new capabilities added to AWS Transform for .NET.

During preview, only projects with public NuGet package dependencies were supported. With general availability, we now support projects with private NuGet package dependencies. This has been one of the most requested features during the preview.

The feature I really love is that AWS Transform can detect cross-repository dependencies. If it finds the source code of my private NuGet package, it automatically transforms that as well. However, if it can’t locate the source code, in the web experience, it provides me the flexibility to upload the required NuGet packages.

AWS Transform displays the missing package dependencies that need to be resolved. There are two ways to do this: I can either use the provided PowerShell script to create and upload packages, or I can build the application locally and upload the NuGet packages from the packages folder in the solution directory.

After I upload the missing NuGet packages, AWS Transform is able to resolve the dependencies. It’s best to provide both the .NET Framework and cross platform .NET versions of the NuGet packages. If the cross platform .NET version is not available, then at a minimum the .NET Framework version is required for AWS Transform to add it as an assembly reference and proceed for transformation.

During preview, we supported porting unit tests from .NET Framework to cross-platform .NET. With general availability, we’ve also added support for executing unit tests after the transformation is complete.

After the transformation is complete and the unit tests are executed, I can see the results in the dashboard and view the status of the tests at each individual test project level.

After the transformation is complete, I can download a detailed report in JSON format that gives me a list of transformed repositories, details about each repository, and the status of the transformation actions performed for each project within a repository. I can view the natural language transformation summary at the project level to understand AWS Transform output with project-level granularity. The summary provides me with an overview of updates along with key technical changes to the codebase.

Let’s have a quick look at other new features we’ve added with general availability:

  • Support for porting UI layer – During preview, you could only port the business logic layers of MVC applications using AWS Transform, and you had to port the UI layer manually. With general availability, you can now use AWS Transform to port MVC Razor views to ASP.NET Core Razor views.
  • Expanded connector support – During preview, you could connect only to GitHub repositories. Now with general availability, you can connect to GitHub, GitLab, and Bitbucket repositories.
  • Cross repository dependency – When you select a repository for transformation, dependent repositories are automatically selected for transformation.
  • Download assessment report – You can download a detailed assessment report of the identified repositories in your account and private NuGet packages referenced in these repositories.
  • Email notifications with deep links – You’ll receive email notifications when a job’s status changes to completed or stopped. These notifications include deep links to the transformed code branches for review and continued transformation in your IDE.

Some additional things to know are:

  • – AWS Transform for .NET is generally available today in the Europe (Frankfurt) and US East (N. Virginia) Regions.
  • – Currently, there is no additional charge for AWS Transform. Any resources you create or continue to use in your AWS account using the output of AWS Transform will be billed according to their standard pricing. For limits and quotas, refer to the documentation.
  • – AWS Transform for .NET supports transforming applications written using .NET Framework versions 3.5+, .NET Core 3.1, and .NET 5+, and the cross-platform .NET version, .NET 8.
  • Application types supported – AWS Transform for .NET supports porting C# code projects of the following types: console application, class library, unit tests, WebAPI, Windows Communication Foundation (WCF) service, MVC, and single-page application (SPA).
  • – To get started, visit AWS Transform for .NET User Guide.

(This survey is hosted by an external company. AWS handles your information as described in the AWS Privacy Notice. AWS will own the data gathered via this survey and will not share the information collected with survey respondents.)

items.title: Why Tailwind CSS Beats Other CSS Frameworks
items.author: Irteza Asad
items.content:
When building websites, choosing the right CSS framework matters. It affects speed, file size, control, and how clean your code looks. Tailwind CSS is a utility-first framework. That means it gives you tiny helper classes to style things directly in your HTML. Sounds weird? Maybe. But it works — and it works better than most.

Let’s break down why Tailwind CSS stands out.

What Makes Tailwind CSS Different

Tailwind doesn’t give you ready-made components like buttons or navbars. Instead, it gives you the building blocks to make your own. Think of it as Lego pieces. You snap together what you need. You don’t start with a full toy that’s hard to change.

This approach gives you full control. You don’t fight against pre-built styles. You style your stuff how you want — without writing custom CSS.

You might think it’ll get messy. All those classes in HTML? But it doesn’t. Once you get used to it, it’s faster, easier, and more flexible than traditional frameworks.

Tailwind vs Bootstrap, Bulma, and Foundation

Let’s compare Tailwind to some big names:

  • Bootstrap gives you pre-styled components like buttons, cards, and modals.
  • You can use them fast, but it’s hard to customize.
  • You often override styles with custom CSS.
  • Your site ends up looking like every other Bootstrap site.
  • The final CSS bundle is huge, with lots of unused styles.

here. It gives you only the styles you use. No bloat. And it doesn’t force design choices on you.

  • Bulma is cleaner than Bootstrap. It’s also component-based.
  • But it still encourages you to follow its design rules.
  • Customizing Bulma is easier than Bootstrap, but not as free as Tailwind.

in flexibility and file size.

  • Foundation is powerful but heavy.
  • It’s packed with JavaScript and styles.
  • It’s not as popular anymore. Learning resources are limited.
  • Customizing it takes time.

in simplicity, community, and speed.

Tailwind is amazing with React

In React, Tailwind fits like a glove. You use the utility classes right inside your JSX:

// JSX code<button className="bg-blue-500 text-white px-4 py-2 rounded"></button>

No extra CSS file. No class name conflicts. You see all the styling in one place. This makes components easy to read and easy to update.

React and Tailwind both support a component-based way of building. You can even make your own reusable components with default styles using Tailwind.

Plus, Tailwind uses a tool called PurgeCSS. When you build your app for production, it looks through your files and removes any CSS classes you didn’t use. Your final CSS bundle includes only what your app needs.

Less code. Faster loading. No waste.

How Tailwind Works With Vue

Tailwind also works great with Vue. You use it just like in React:

// Vue Template<template>
  <div class="p-6 max-w-sm bg-white rounded shadow">
    <h1 class="text-xl font-bold">Hello, Vue</h1></template>

Vue templates keep your HTML and Tailwind classes together. This makes it easy to see your layout and design at once.

Tailwind’s tree-shaking (removing unused CSS) also works with Vue. You just point it to your .vue files, and it’ll clean up your CSS. The final output only includes what you actually use.

Vue’s component system matches well with Tailwind’s utility-first style. You can build design systems quickly and reuse components with confidence.

At first, it might look like more typing. But over time, Tailwind saves hours. You don’t switch between CSS and HTML files. You don’t invent new class names every time. You don’t debug “why isn’t this class working?”

You just write what you need — right where you need it.

Need text bold? Use font-bold.

There’s a class for almost everything, and btw, did I mention Tailwind has one of the best documentation I’ve come across.

A Few Downsides (But Worth It)

Let’s be honest. Tailwind isn’t perfect.

  • It has a learning curve at first.
  • Your HTML can look busy with lots of classes.
  • You need to configure it if you want custom colors, spacing, or fonts.

But once you set it up, it's a gem!

Tailwind CSS isn’t just another tool. It’s a different way to build websites. You stop thinking in terms of components someone else designed. You build your own, faster and lighter.

If you want speed, control, and clean code — Tailwind is the way to go. Bootstrap and others served us well, but Tailwind is better for today’s web.

It’s not hype. It’s just smart.

items.title: The Ultimate Guide to Autocomplete: Make Your Forms Smarter (With Zero Effort)
items.author: Irteza Asad
items.content:
Filling out forms is nobody’s idea of fun. But what if your website could predict what users need to type — before they even finish? That’s where HTML’s autocomplete attribute comes in.

Most developers know the basics (like autocomplete="email"), but there’s a whole world of autocomplete values that can turbocharge your forms. From credit cards to one-time codes, here’s how to use them all.

1. One-Time Codes (The iOS Shortcut)

Back in 2018, Apple introduced this nifty feature at WWDC. If your website sets up its input field the right way, iOS will detect the SMS code and offer to autofill it. No extra work required — just pure HTML magic.

<input    type="text"    inputmode="numeric"    autocomplete="one-time-code"

Login forms, 2FA, password resets

2. Contact Info (Emails, Names, Phone Numbers)

Just to cover the basics — you’re already familiar with these

  • autocomplete="email" – Autofills saved emails.
  • autocomplete="name" – Full name.
  • autocomplete="tel" – Phone number.
<input type="email" autocomplete="email" />  <input type="text" autocomplete="name" />  
<input type="tel" autocomplete="tel" />

💡 Use autocomplete="username" for logins (works with password managers).

3. Address Autofill (Bye-Bye, Tedious Forms)

Filling out addresses is the worst. Fix it with,

  • autocomplete="street-address" – Full address.
  • autocomplete="postal-code" – ZIP/postal code.
  • autocomplete="country" – Country name
<input autocomplete="street-address" />  <input autocomplete="postal-code" />  
<select autocomplete="country">  
  <option>Afghanistan</option>    <!-- ... -->

Some browsers even auto-format postal codes!

4. Payment & Credit Card Autofill

If your site takes payments, never make users type card details manually again.

  • autocomplete="cc-number" – Credit card number.
  • autocomplete="cc-exp" – Expiration date (MM/YY).
  • autocomplete="cc-csc" – CVV/CVC security code.
<input autocomplete="cc-number" placeholder="Card number" />  <input autocomplete="cc-exp" placeholder="MM/YY" />  
<input autocomplete="cc-csc" placeholder="CVV" />

⚠️ Always use HTTPS for payment fields!

5. Passwords (The Ultimate Time-Saver)

Password managers (like iCloud Keychain or Chrome’s built-in) rely on autocomplete to save and fill credentials.

  • autocomplete="current-password" – For login forms.
  • autocomplete="new-password" – For signups/password resets.
<input type="password" autocomplete="current-password" />

🔒 autocomplete="off" on passwords—it breaks manager support!

6. Super Niche (But Useful) Autofills

  • autocomplete="transaction-amount" – For payment amounts.
  • autocomplete="bday" – Birthdays (formatted YYYY-MM-DD).
  • autocomplete="photo" – For file uploads (rarely supported).

When Not to Use Autocomplete

Autofill is a game-changer for UX, but there are times when it  — either by being annoying, insecure, or just plain wrong. Here’s when you should with autocomplete="off":

1. Search Boxes (Unless You Want History Clutter)

Browsers love autofilling past searches, which can be distracting (or embarrassing) if users don’t want them showing up.

<input type="search" autocomplete="off" />

2. Sensitive Admin/Internal Fields

Fields like (mother’s maiden name), , or should be saved by browsers.

<input type="text" autocomplete="off" placeholder="Security question answer" />

⚠️ Browsers sometimes ignore off for passwords—use autocomplete="new-password" instead for reset forms.

3. Unique or One-Time Inputs

Fields like , , or temporary survey responses don’t benefit from autofill — and might confuse users if saved.

<input type="text" autocomplete="off" placeholder="Promo code (one-time use)" />

4. Custom or Non-Standard Fields

If your form has (e.g., “Favorite movie quote” or “Internal project code”), autofill might suggest  values.

<input type="text" autocomplete="off" placeholder="Employee badge number" />

5. Multi-Step Forms (Where Context Changes)

If a form spans multiple steps (like a checkout flow), autofill might pre-populate fields too early with outdated info.

<!-- Step 1: Shipping address (autofill OK) -->  <input autocomplete="shipping street-address" />  
<!-- Step 2: Gift message (disable autofill) -->  
<textarea autocomplete="off" placeholder="Write a note..."></textarea>

6. Fields That Trigger Unwanted Actions

Autofill can sometimes (like login fields auto-triggering on page load).

<form autocomplete="off"> <!-- Disable for entire form if needed -->    <input type="text" name="token" />

Autocomplete isn’t just a convenience — it’s a . By using the right values, you:✔️ (and frustration).✔️ (especially in long forms).✔️ Work with password managers (better security).

Best part? No JavaScript needed. So

items.title: Automate Script Deployment and Scheduling with Ansible
items.author: Kartik Dudeja
items.content:
In the world of DevOps, automation is not just a convenience — it’s a superpower. And when it comes to configuring remote systems at scale, continues to shine as one of the most elegant and efficient tools in the toolbox.

In this article, we’ll walk through an Ansible playbook designed to automate two crucial tasks:

  1. Deploying a shell script to remote servers, and
  2. Scheduling it with crontab so it runs at regular intervals.

📜 Anatomy of the Playbook

Here’s the heart of the automation:

- name: Deploy Script and Make a Crontab Entry  hosts: all  gather_facts: false

We begin by defining a playbook that runs on , with become: true to elevate privileges when needed. No facts gathering means faster execution, since we're dealing with static tasks.

vars:  - SCRIPT_NAME: <script-name>  - SCRIPT_DEST: <destination-path-on-remote-server>
  - SCRIPT_USER_OWNER: "centos"
  - SCRIPT_GROUP_OWNER: "centos"
  - DEPLOY_FOR_USER: "centos"
  - MINUTE: "*/10"  - DAY: "*"  - WEEKDAY: "*"

These variables give you full control over where and how your script is deployed, and at what schedule it should run. Want it every hour? Change MINUTE: 0. Different user? Change DEPLOY_FOR_USER, SCRIPT_USER_OWNER, SCRIPT_GROUP_OWNER.

📂 Task 1: Ensure the Directory Exists

- name: "Create the directory if it does not exist"  ansible.builtin.file:
    path: "{{ SCRIPT_DEST }}"
    state: directory

Simple and effective — if the target directory isn’t there, it’s created.

📄 Task 2: Copy the Script

- name: "Place Script at the desired path"  ansible.builtin.copy:
    src: "{{ SCRIPT_SRC }}/{{ SCRIPT_NAME }}"
    dest: "{{ SCRIPT_DEST }}/{{ SCRIPT_NAME }}"
    owner: "{{ SCRIPT_USER_OWNER }}"
    group: "{{ SCRIPT_GROUP_OWNER }}"

⏰ Task 3: Add a Crontab Entry

- name: "make crontab entry for the script"  ansible.builtin.cron:
    name: "Test Script deployed by Ansible"
    job: "{{ SCRIPT_DEST }}/{{ SCRIPT_NAME }}"
    user: "{{ DEPLOY_FOR_USER }}"
    minute: "{{ MINUTE }}"    day: "{{ DAY }}"    weekday: "{{ WEEKDAY }}"

This is where the magic happens. A cron job is created (or updated if it already exists) to schedule the script’s execution. With the default values, it will run every 10 minutes.

# Deploy Bash Script with Ansible
- name: Deploy Script and Make a Crontab Entry
  hosts: all  gather_facts: false
  # update these variable as per the need
  vars:  - SCRIPT_NAME: <script-name>  - SCRIPT_DEST: <destination-path-on-remote-server>
  - SCRIPT_USER_OWNER: "centos"
  - SCRIPT_GROUP_OWNER: "centos"  - DEPLOY_FOR_USER: "centos"  - HOUR: "*"  - MONTH: "*"
  # create operations script directory if it doesn't exist already
  - name: "Create the directory if it does not exist"      path: "{{ SCRIPT_DEST }}"      mode: '0755'
  # copy the script on the host
  - name: "Place Script at the desired path"      src: "{{ SCRIPT_SRC }}/{{ SCRIPT_NAME }}"
      dest: "{{ SCRIPT_DEST }}/{{ SCRIPT_NAME }}"
      owner: "{{ SCRIPT_USER_OWNER }}"
      group: "{{ SCRIPT_GROUP_OWNER }}"
      mode: '0755'  - name: "make crontab entry for the script"      name: "Test Script deployed by Ansible"
      job: "{{ SCRIPT_DEST }}/{{ SCRIPT_NAME }}"
      user: "{{ DEPLOY_FOR_USER }}"      hour: "{{ HOUR }}"      month: "{{ MONTH }}"
  1. as deploy_script.yml
  2. in the same directory
  3. if needed (script name, user, schedule)
ansible-playbook -i inventory.ini deploy_script.yml

Make sure your inventory.ini file lists the remote hosts you want to target.

  • : Run it anytime to redeploy or update the cron schedule.
  • : Works across any number of hosts defined in your inventory.
  • : Ansible ensures tasks are only applied when necessary.

Whether you’re deploying monitoring scripts, maintenance tasks, or log rotators, this playbook gives you a clean and automated pathway.

items.title: Mastering CI/CD with GitHub Actions: The Essentials (Part 1)
items.author: Houssem Kraoua
items.content:
Ever felt overwhelmed by repetitive tasks in your software development process? GitHub Actions can help you automate everything from testing and building to app deployment — no hassle, no headache. Let’s dive into how you can automate your workflows with GitHub Actions, starting from the basics and building up to more sophisticated automation.

In this blog series, we’ll guide you through setting up and optimizing a CI/CD pipeline, using GitHub Actions as the backbone of your automation. From running tests and controlling environments to deploying and safeguarding your workflows, we’ll cover it all. But first, let’s lay the foundation, and you’ll be automating like a pro in no time!

Assume you are building a factory to complete tasks. Like the manufacturing schedule, the workflow tells it what chores to do, when to do them, and in what sequence.

A Github Actions is a set of instructions (written in a .yml or .yamlfile) that defines what needs to happen in your project. Whether it's running tests, building your app, or deploying it, among many other things. It's the entire process, the big picture that ties everything together.

Is there a different types of This Workflows?

Just like a factory can have different , a can be triggered in different ways. These are the  — the various ways you can execute a workflow, depending on your needs.

You can trigger a workflow in different ways, like:

  • : A production line starts when raw materials (code) are pushed to the factory (your GitHub repository).
  • : The production line kicks off when you receive a request to combine two parts (branches).
  • : The factory runs automatically at a specific time, like clockwork, for periodic tasks.
  • : The factory’s production line only starts when you presses a button to begin the process.
  • Repository Dispatch Workflow: The factory has a remote control system that allows you to trigger the production line from another factory or external system.

But sometimes, before your factory starts working, it needs clear instructions on what exactly to do. Maybe it needs to know which product to make or which materials to use.

Inputs: Special Instructions for the Factory

Before starting, sometimes the factory needs clear instructions on what to do. For example, it might need to know which product to make or which materials to use. These instructions are called in GitHub Actions.

    Inputs provide specific instructions for the workflow without changing the design of the workflow itself.

    Turning the Plan into Action

    Now, let’s look at how everything comes to life inside the factory using , , and .

    Jobs: The Heart of the Factory

    A is like the main machine in the factory — it does one or more tasks and runs independently. For example, one job could be to assemble a product or inspect its quality.

    Steps: The Gears That Make It Move

    Each is like a gear inside the machine. It’s a small, individual task that contributes to the main job. The steps work together to make sure the job runs smoothly.

    Actions: The Special Tools

    An is like a specialized tool that the machine uses to complete a specific task. GitHub Actions provides reusable and pre-built actions that can be plugged into your steps to help automate tasks.

    Still not clear? Let’s do a quick recap:
    • = The factory blueprint (defines the entire process).
    • = Different production lines that start based on events (push, pull request, schedule, manual)
    • : Instructions for this specific run.
    • = The heart of the factory.
    • = The gears inside the machines (jobs).
    • = Reusable tools that help tasks get done.

    The Workers: GitHub Runners

    Now that we know about the machines, gears, and tools, let’s talk about the workers who make everything happen: .

    Runners are the employees in the factory who execute the jobs, follow the steps, and use the actions to get the work done. Without them, the workflow wouldn’t function.

    As of now there are two main types of runners and understanding the difference is key:

    1. GitHub-Hosted Runners: Your Out-of-the-Box Factory Workers

    GitHub-hosted runners are virtual machines pre-configured by GitHub to help you get started quickly. They come with popular tools like Node.js, Python, Docker, and more.

    ✅ every time✅ No setup required.

    GitHub gives you some for Ubuntu, Windows and Macos but in order to use them depends on your account:

    • Public Repos (All Tiers): Unlimited usage — go wild!
    • Private Repos(Free Tier): 2,000 minutes/month + 500MB storage.
    • Paid Plans (Team/Enterprise): Higher or unlimited limits based on the plan.

    Free Github runners are great choice to start withespecially if you’re working on open-source projects or just getting started.

    For or that need more power, flexibility, or additional resources, GitHub offers a wide range of powerful runners as part of its as they willprovide you more resources, and you’ll get higher usage limits.

    The Ones You Hire

    These are runners that you set up yourself, such as on your own machine or cloud instances on Azure or AWS. They offer more control over your environment and are useful for custom setups, persistent environments, or working with private resources.

    ✅ More control over the environment.✅ Persistent or customized setup.

    When setting up a runner, GitHub lets you choose between two places to install it:

    📁 Used for a single repository, perfect for project-specific tasks. Set it up in the repo’s settings.

    🏢 Organization-Level Runner: Shared across multiple repositories in your organization, ideal for team-wide use. Set it up in the organization’s settings.

    In this first part we will just use GitHub’s free public runner, so you won’t need to set up a runner yourself.

    Now that you understand the core concepts, let’s bring everything to life with a simple, working example.

    Setting Up Your First Workflow

    Let’s put everything together with a simple workflow! To begin, you need to set up the right structure in your project:

    1. in the root of your project.
    2. inside .github.
    3. Create your first workflow file: Inside the workflows folder, create a file like simple-workflow.yml.

    Once that’s in place, you’re ready to define your first automation!

    name: Simple Workflowon:    inputs:        description: 'Which environment to deploy to? (e.g., dev or prod)'        type: string  simple-job:    steps:        uses: actions/checkout@v4
          - name: Print Environment
            run: echo "Deploying to ${{ github.event.inputs.environment }} environment"
    1. : This gives a name to your workflow (e.g., “Simple Workflow”) and its the name that will show on the actions Tab UI.
    2. : This defines how the workflow is triggered. Here, we use workflow_dispatch, which means the workflow can be started manually from the GitHub Actions UI.
    3. we define a required input called environment. This allows the user to specify (e.g., dev or prod) when triggering the workflow.
    4. : This is the “engine” of our workflow, where the actual tasks are defined. In this case, we have one job named simple-job.
    5. Specifies the type of virtual machine (runner) to use. Here, we will use GitHub’s Free Ubuntu runner (ubuntu-latest).
    6. : These are the gears inside the job, defining the individual tasks to execute:
    • : This uses the actions/checkout@v4 action to pull down your repository’s latest code. (It's like running git clone in the runner.)
    • step 2 — Print Environment: A simple step that prints the selected environment input to the console using echo.

    Before you can run any workflows, make sure GitHub Actions is enabled for your repository. Most of the time, it’s on by default — but it’s always good to double-check.

    1. Go to your GitHub repository.
    2. In the left sidebar, select .
    3. Under , make sure Allow all actions and reusable workflows is selected.
    💡 Want to enable Actions for your entire organization? Just follow the same steps from your org’s .

    ▶️ But Now… How Should I Run It?

    Once you’ve pushed your changes and ensured GitHub Actions is enabled:

    1. Go back to your GitHub .
    2. Select your workflow name from the left sidebar (e.g., ).
    3. Click the  button.
    4. Choose the desired input (like environment) from the dropdown.
    5. Hit again — and you’re off!

    🎉 You’ve just set up your first GitHub Actions workflow!

    Now that you’ve set up a basic workflow, you’re ready to explore even more powerful automation with GitHub Actions! In the next part of this series, we’ll dive into:

    • 💥 Break our workflow into smart, connected jobs
    • 🧪 Automate testing and Docker image builds
    • 🔐 Securely manage secrets and environment variables
    • 🤖 Introduce GitHub Actions Marketplace (aka your robot factory floor!)

    You’ll learn how to scale your pipeline like a real production line — with clean separation, secure access, and reusable tools. Let’s turn your automation into a well-oiled machine!

    Stay tuned as we continue building and automating your app, from start to finish! 🚀

items.title: GCP Workload Identity Federation
items.author: Mohamed Rasvi
items.content:
Google Cloud Platform (GCP) offers a robust set of tools, but some of its most powerful features remain underutilized due to lackluster marketing and sparse documentation. Workload Identity Federation (WIF) is one such gem, enabling secure, keyless authentication for external systems like GitHub Actions to access GCP APIs without the risks of long-lived credentials like service account keys. By directly mapping external identities to GCP IAM roles, WIF eliminates the need for intermediary service accounts, streamlining security for CI/CD pipelines. However, its complexity and limited guidance have kept it obscure. This article explores WIF’s untapped potential, critiques GCP’s failure to promote it, and provides a step-by-step guide to implement it within a GCP organization for secure GitHub Actions deployments.

The Problem: GCP’s Missed Opportunity with WIF

Workload Identity Federation is a stellar security tool, yet it’s barely a blip on GCP’s marketing radar. Unlike flagship services like BigQuery or Cloud Run, WIF lacks the visibility it deserves. GCP’s documentation, while technically detailed, is dense and assumes familiarity with OpenID Connect (OIDC) and IAM, leaving the average developer let’s call them “Joe” struggling to get started. Practical tutorials are scarce, and the setup process feels like navigating a maze.

This oversight is costly. WIF’s ability to directly authenticate external identities to GCP resources without service account keys addresses a critical security pain point: credential management. By not promoting WIF or providing clear, beginner-friendly guides, GCP has let a killer product languish in obscurity. Compare this to AWS’s OIDC integration for GitHub Actions, which enjoys better documentation and community traction, and it’s clear GCP needs to rethink its approach.

Why Workload Identity Federation Shines for Security

Traditional GCP authentication for CI/CD pipelines often involves service account keys — JSON files with static credentials that are prone to leaks if mismanaged. Workload Identity Federation bypasses this by using to directly map external identities (e.g., GitHub Actions) to GCP IAM principals. This approach is a security game-changer for several reasons:

  • : WIF uses ephemeral tokens (valid for ~5 minutes), drastically reducing the risk of credential exposure.
  • : External identities can be granted IAM roles directly, eliminating the need for service accounts and simplifying permission management.
  • : Permissions are scoped to specific resources and repositories, ensuring least-privilege access.
  • : WIF integrates seamlessly with CI/CD pipelines, supporting deployments across multiple GCP projects.

For organizations building secure CI/CD pipelines, WIF’s keyless, direct authentication is a must-have. Yet, its steep learning curve and lack of clear documentation have kept it out of the mainstream.

Implementing WIF in a GCP Organization Structure

To showcase WIF’s power, let’s walk through setting it up within a GCP organization for a CI/CD pipeline using GitHub Actions. We’ll focus on direct authentication, granting GitHub identities IAM roles without service accounts.

Step 1: Define the GCP Organization Structure

GCP organizes resources hierarchically: an contains , which house . For a CI/CD setup:

  1. : Use Cloud Identity or Google Workspace to create a GCP Organization, the root node for resource management.
  2. : Organize projects logically. For example, a “CI/CD” folder for pipeline resources and a “Production” folder for live applications.
  3. : Under the CI/CD folder, create a project (e.g., cicd-pipeline) for pipeline configuration. Add projects like app-prod and app-dev under other folders for application deployments.

This structure enables centralized IAM policies at the organization or folder level, with projects inheriting permissions.

Step 2: Configure Workload Identity Federation

WIF uses a and to establish trust between GCP and GitHub Actions. Here’s how to set it up for direct authentication:

Create a Workload Identity Pool:

  • In the GCP Console, go to IAM & Admin > Workload Identity Federation.
  • Create a pool named github-cicd-pool.
  • Or use the gcloud CLI for automation:
gcloud iam workload-identity-pools create github-cicd-pool \   --location="global" \   
--display-name="GitHub CI/CD Pool"

Add a GitHub OIDC Provider:

gcloud iam workload-identity-pools providers create-oidc github-provider \  --location="global" \
  --workload-identity-pool="github-cicd-pool" \
  --issuer-uri="https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com/" \
  --attribute-mapping="google.subject=assertion.sub,attribute.repository=assertion.repository" \
  --attribute-condition="assertion.repository=='your-org/your-repo'"
  • Note the provider’s full path: projects/YOUR_PROJECT_NUMBER/locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/github-cicd-pool/providers/github-provider

:

  • Instead of linking a service account, bind IAM roles directly to the workload identity pool principal.
  • For example, grant roles/storage.admin to the GitHub repository’s identity for Cloud Storage access in the app-prod project:
gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding app-prod \   --member="principalSet://iam.googleapis.com/projects/YOUR_PROJECT_NUMBER/locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/github-cicd-pool/attribute.repository/your-org/your-repo" \   
--role="roles/storage.admin"
  • Replace YOUR_PROJECT_NUMBER with the numeric ID of the cicd-pipeline project hosting the identity pool.

Step 3: Set Up GitHub Actions for Direct Authentication

Configure your GitHub Actions workflow to authenticate to GCP using WIF’s OIDC tokens:

:

  • Use the google-github-actions/auth action to exchange GitHub’s OIDC token for GCP access.
  • Example workflow to deploy files to Cloud Storage:
name: Deploy to GCPon:    branches: [ main ]  deploy:    permissions:      id-token: 'write'    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    - uses: google-github-actions/auth@v2
      with:
        workload_identity_provider: 'projects/YOUR_PROJECT_NUMBER/locations/global/workloadIdentityPools/github-cicd-pool/providers/github-provider'
    - run: gcloud storage cp ./my-app/* gs://my-bucket/
  • Push a change to the main branch to trigger the workflow.
  • GitHub Actions generates an OIDC token, which GCP validates against the workload identity provider, granting access to the specified resources.

Step 4: Scale Across Projects

To deploy to multiple projects (e.g., app-prod, app-dev):

  • Add IAM policy bindings for the workload identity principal in each target project, granting roles like roles/compute.admin for Compute Engine.
  • Update the GitHub Actions workflow to target different projects using the project_id parameter or environment variables:
  • - run: gcloud storage cp ./my-app/* gs://my-bucket/ --project=app-prod
  • Use folder-level IAM policies to apply permissions across multiple projects, ensuring consistent access for the CI/CD pipeline.

Challenges and Limitations

Implementing WIF without service accounts is powerful but not without hurdles:

  • : Configuring attribute mappings and direct IAM bindings requires understanding OIDC and GCP IAM, which can intimidate newcomers.
  • : GitHub’s OIDC tokens expire in ~5 minutes, requiring workflows to execute quickly or implement token refresh logic.
  • : GCP’s guides rarely cover direct principal-based WIF, and examples for GitHub Actions are even harder to find.
  • : Direct IAM bindings can become unwieldy for large organizations, requiring careful role assignments to avoid over-privileging.

These challenges highlight the need for better documentation and tools to simplify WIF adoption.

To boost WIF’s adoption, GCP should:

  • : Offer clear, step-by-step guides for direct WIF authentication, including GitHub Actions and other CI/CD platforms.
  • : Feature WIF in security and DevOps campaigns, emphasizing its keyless, direct authentication benefits.
  • : Develop CLI tools or Console wizards to automate identity pool setup and IAM bindings.
  • : Encourage blogs, videos, and tutorials to showcase WIF’s real-world applications.

items.title: Ambatuscrum #9: Monitorama: Taming Your Django Dragons
items.author: Soros F
items.content:
Modern web applications demand more than just functionality — they must be reliable, fast, and transparent when things go wrong. In this post, you’ll learn how to architect a monitoring stack that covers:

  1. Built‑in platform monitoring (Firebase, GCP, AWS, DigitalOcean)
  2. for error, log, user‑activity, and performance monitoring (Sentry, ELK/Cloud Logging, Google Analytics, New Relic)
  3. for your own business logic (e.g. tracking critical function calls)
  4. : dashboards, alerts, and continuous improvement

We’ll illustrate with a real‑world example: instrumenting a Django REST Framework (DRF) service running in Docker, using Prometheus and Grafana.

1. Why Monitoring Matters

Imagine a customer filling out a form on your site — then suddenly nothing happens. Did the server crash? Was there a network spike? Without proper monitoring, you’re blind. With it, you can:

  • incidents in real time
  • root causes by correlating errors, logs, and performance metrics
  • resource usage and user experience
  • the team before SLAs are breached

2. Leverage Built‑In Platform Monitoring

Most cloud and mobile platforms ship with free monitoring “for free”:

Activate these out of the box. Even if you plan to deploy third‑party tools later, you’ll have a baseline.

3. Standard Monitoring Stack

A healthy observability stack typically includes four pillars:

3.1 Error Monitoring with Sentry

  • : Install the Sentry SDK in Django (sentry-sdk[django]), configure DSN.
  • to capture real client IP in Sentry
# myapp/middleware.pyfrom ipware import get_client_ip
class ClientIPMiddleware:
    def __init__(self, get_response):
        self.get_response = get_response
    def __call__(self, request):
        client_ip, _ = get_client_ip(request)
        real_port = request.META.get("HTTP_X_REAL_PORT")
            # attach “ip_address” field to every Sentry event                "ip_address": real_port and f"{client_ip}:{real_port}"            })
        return self.get_response(request)
# settings.pyimport sentry_sdk
from sentry_sdk.integrations.django import DjangoIntegration
    dsn=os.environ["SENTRY_DSN"],
    integrations=[DjangoIntegration()],
    traces_sample_rate=0.2,       # adjust for performance tracing
    send_default_pii=True,        # include user context from middleware
)    # …
    "myapp.middleware.ClientIPMiddleware",
    # …
  • : Every unhandled exception (and breadcrumbs leading up to it) appears in Sentry with user context.

This view combines APM (transactions) and query‑level performance data collected by the Sentry Python SDK.

A. Most Time‑Consuming Queries

  • : SQL statements ranked by total time spent executing.
  • : Reveals hotspots in your ORM or raw SQL that slow responses (e.g. heavy INSERT INTO silk_request … or slow UPDATE statements).
  • : Add indexes, batch writes, or reduce fields logged if these queries are not business‑critical.

B. Most Time‑Consuming Domains

  • : External HTTP calls (by domain) ranked by total latency.
  • : Third‑party API calls (e.g. to *.ui.ac.id:8080) can dominate your request time.
  • : Cache responses, add retries with backoff, or move calls into background jobs.

C. Transactions Per Minute

  • : Number of traced transactions (i.e. instrumented Django views/endpoints) per minute.
  • : Correlate throughput with error spikes or latency changes.
  • : If TPS jumps but p95 latency stays flat, your autoscaling is healthy; if latency also jumps, investigate resource constraints.
  • : The 50th‑ and 75th‑percentile durations of your transactions over the selected time window.
  • : p50 shows typical user experience; p75 begins to expose the “slower quartile.”
  • : Set alert thresholds on p75 or p95 to catch regressions before they impact most users.
  • : Ship your Django logs (JSON‑formatted) to Elasticsearch or Grafana Loki.
  • : You can query “which endpoints log ERROR most often?” and pivot on request parameters.

3.3 User Activity (Google Analytics)

  • : Add the GA4 snippet to your frontend (or server‑side track events via Measurement Protocol).
  • : Understand user flows, drop‑off points, and conversion funnels.

3.4 Platform Performance (New Relic)

  • : Install the New Relic agent in your Python process, set your license key.
  • : Get out‑of‑the‑box APM dashboards showing throughput, transaction traces, database query times.

4. Case Study: Prometheus & Grafana on Dockerized DRF

Here’s how I wired up Prometheus + Grafana to my Django REST Framework app in Docker. Within minutes I had real‑time metrics flowing.

4.1 Docker‑Compose Overview

$ docker ps -aCONTAINER ID   IMAGE                                STATUS       PORTS
f50d645d10eb   myapp-be-staging:latest             Up 37h       8001→8000
27a244b363cd   prom/prometheus                     Up 7h        9090→9090
b3bce01bde5a   grafana/grafana                     Up 6d        3002→3000
…

4.2 Prometheus Configuration (prometheus.yml)

global:  scrape_interval: 15s  - job_name: 'prometheus'      - targets: ['localhost:9090']
- job_name: 'singawas-be-production'
    metrics_path: /metrics/      - targets: ['myapp-be-production:8000']
  - job_name: 'singawas-be-dev'
    metrics_path: /metrics/      - targets: ['myapp-be-staging:8000']
  - job_name: 'singawas-fe'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['myapp:3000']
remote_write:
  - url: https://your-remote-write-endpoint
    basic_auth:      password: ***
Two back‑end jobs (production & staging) scraping /metrics/ from DRF’s Prometheus exporter
Front‑end jobs to track overall request counts (if instrumented)
Remote write to a central metrics store

4.3 Grafana Dashboard Highlights

  • Below are the panels from your “Simple Django” and “Django” dashboards, with the PromQL (or concept) behind each and why it matters.
  • : total HTTP requests per second (or minute) across all endpoints
sum(rate(http_requests_total[1m]))
  • : baseline throughput — spikes may indicate heavy load or abuse.

2. 2XX / 4XX / 5XX Responses

  • : break‑out of successful vs. client‑error vs. server‑error rates
sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"2.."}[1m])) sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"4.."}[1m])) 
sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[1m]))
  • : rising 4XX might signal bad client calls; rising 5XX is a server‑failure alert.

3. Request Latency (p50, p95, p99)

  • : distribution of request durations
histogram_quantile(0.50, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le)) histogram_quantile(0.95, …) 
histogram_quantile(0.99, …)
  • : p95/p99 show your “tail latency” — critical for SLAs and user experience.
  • : CPU seconds consumed per second by your app containers
rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{container="myapp-be-staging"}[1m])
  • : sudden jumps can indicate inefficient code paths or traffic storms.
  • : memory footprint of your Django process
container_memory_working_set_bytes{container="myapp-be-staging"}
  • : creeping memory use may herald a leak.
  • : request count per route (e.g. /api/v1/orders/)
sum(rate(http_requests_total[1m])) by (handler)
  • : identifies your hottest endpoints; focus optimization where it counts.
  • : whether Prometheus can scrape each target (1 = up, 0 = down)
up{job="singawas-be-production"}
  • : instant red/green health indicator.

8. Transactions Per Minute (Backend insights)

  • : total “transactions” (Sentry APM spans) per minute
  • : correlate to request load and error spikes.

9. p50 / p75 Duration (Backend insights)

  • : median and 75th‑percentile transaction duration from Sentry APM
  • : complements Prometheus histograms with end‑to‑end tracing data.

5. Advanced Usage: Alerts & Custom Dashboards

5.1 Alerting with Prometheus

groups:- name: app-alerts  - alert: HighErrorRate
    expr: increase(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m]) > 5
    for: 2m      severity: critical      summary: "High 5xx error rate detected"
      description: "More than 5 server errors in the last 5 minutes."
  • to Slack, email, or PagerDuty via Alertmanager
  • : Your team wakes up before customers do.
  • Define thresholds on latency panels (e.g. p95 > 500 ms)
  • Trigger notifications to Microsoft Teams, OpsGenie, etc.
  • Blend Sentry error counts with Prometheus metrics
  • Overlay user‑flow drop‑offs (GA) on performance graphs
  • Build “service health” views for on‑call engineers

6. Putting It All Together

By closing the loop — from zero visibility to full‑stack observability — you ensure your application is resilient and your team is empowered to act.

  1. your custom metrics (database pool usage, cache hit rates)
  2. on‑call runbooks via alert annotations
  3. logs, traces (Jaeger), and metrics for distributed tracing
  4. dashboards with stakeholders for data‑driven decision making

With this foundation, you’ve transformed raw data into actionable insights — making performance, reliability, and user satisfaction a continuous, measurable journey.

Enjoyed this guide? If you try these steps in your own project, drop a comment or share your Grafana dashboards!

items.title: From Monolith to Microservices: A Step-by-Step Guide for Seamless Transition
items.author: Anton Baksheiev
items.content:
How to Seamlessly Shift from a Monolithic Architecture to Independent Microservices

Breaking a into can be a complex process that requires careful planning, execution, and often a cultural shift within the organization. The goal is to split the monolith into smaller, independently deployable, and scalable services, each responsible for a specific business functionality. Here’s a general step-by-step approach to help you transition from a monolithic system to microservices:

1. Understand the Monolith

  • Map the current architecture: Before breaking anything, thoroughly understand how the monolithic system is structured. Identify major modules, dependencies, and components that interact with each other.
  • Identify critical business domains: Look for different business functionalities that could form the basis for separate microservices. These might be modules like , , , etc.

2. Define Clear Boundaries (Domain-Driven Design)

  • Domain-driven design (DDD): Start by defining service boundaries based on business domains. This is one of the key principles in microservices. By breaking the monolith into services that represent , you ensure that each service is cohesive and responsible for a well-defined task.
  • : Ensure that the boundaries for each microservice are clearly defined, meaning each service manages its own data and logic.

3. Start with the Core Services

  • Extract one service at a time: It’s best to by picking one of the least complex, self-contained components (such as user management or product catalog) to migrate first.
  • : If your monolith uses a single database, start by splitting the database schema or transitioning to multiple databases — one for each microservice. Each microservice should manage its own database to avoid tight coupling.
  • : Microservices should communicate via well-defined APIs, typically over or . You’ll need to expose public endpoints for the service you are migrating.

4. Use a Strangler Fig Pattern

  • : The is an incremental approach where the old monolith and new microservices coexist. Over time, you “strangle” the monolith by replacing parts of it with microservices.
  • : You might set up a to route traffic to the appropriate microservice and legacy system, allowing you to migrate features gradually.
  • : During the migration, the monolith may still be running, but new features are developed as microservices.

5. Ensure Service Communication

  • Synchronous communication: Microservices can communicate with each other using , , or for synchronous calls.
  • Asynchronous communication: For better decoupling and scalability, use message queues or event streaming platforms (e.g., , ) for asynchronous communication. This is useful for handling events and background tasks like , , etc.

6. Address Data Management and Consistency

  • : In a monolithic architecture, you typically have a single database, but in microservices, each service often manages its own database. This introduces challenges with data consistency, so you might need to implement patterns using event-driven approaches.
  • : For managing across multiple services, you can implement the . This helps coordinate long-running transactions, ensuring that they’re completed or compensated.

7. Build Independent Deployment Pipelines

  • : Each microservice should have its own for continuous integration and delivery. This ensures each service can be developed, tested, and deployed independently, without affecting others.
  • : Use to package each microservice into a container, and or another orchestration tool to manage service deployment, scaling, and networking.

8. Handle Authentication and Authorization

  • Centralized authentication: If your monolith uses a central authentication system, consider migrating to an authentication service like or tokens for each service. Implement a or centralized identity management to maintain security across services.
  • : Implement an to manage cross-cutting concerns like authentication, rate limiting, and routing requests to the correct microservice.

9. Monitor and Scale Services Independently

  • : Implement centralized monitoring and logging (using tools like , , , or ) for all microservices. This is crucial to identify and troubleshoot issues quickly as the system scales.
  • : Each microservice should be independently scalable based on demand. This allows your system to better handle increased load and optimize resource usage.
  • Unit and integration tests: Microservices require thorough testing at both the and levels. Each service should be independently tested, and their interactions should also be tested.
  • : Conduct to ensure that the system works as expected when the microservices communicate with each other.

11. Decommission the Monolith

  • Once the migration is complete and all critical business functionalities have been moved to microservices, you can gradually decommission the monolith.
  • Make sure to monitor the system closely during this phase to address any unforeseen issues that may arise as legacy features are removed.

Challenges in the Process:

  • : As you migrate, managing data consistency across microservices can become complex. Tools like or CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) may help.
  • : Managing many services can increase operational complexity, which is why strong tooling (like Kubernetes, CI/CD, and monitoring) is essential.
  • : Moving to microservices often involves adopting a DevOps culture, where teams work more independently and take ownership of specific services.

Migrating from a monolithic architecture to microservices is a complex yet rewarding process that requires careful planning, clear boundaries, and incremental changes. By understanding the current monolith and identifying key business domains, you lay the foundation for a seamless transition. Domain-Driven Design (DDD) helps define clear service boundaries, ensuring each microservice is cohesive and responsible for its own data.

Starting with core services allows you to manage the complexity step by step, using patterns like the Strangler Fig for a gradual, controlled migration. Ensuring robust communication between services, both synchronous and asynchronous, is essential for smooth operation in a distributed environment.

Addressing challenges such as data consistency and implementing efficient service communication mechanisms, like event-driven patterns and APIs, will help maintain reliability and scalability. Moreover, building independent deployment pipelines and ensuring thorough testing at all stages ensures that each microservice can evolve independently without disrupting the overall system.

The shift to microservices requires a cultural change, as teams take ownership of specific services and embrace a DevOps mindset. Monitoring, scaling independently, and decommissioning the monolith gradually are key aspects to keep the system running smoothly during and after the transition.

While the process brings increased complexity and challenges, the long-term benefits, such as scalability, flexibility, and faster development cycles, make it a worthwhile endeavor. By taking a structured and careful approach, businesses can unlock the full potential of microservices and create a more resilient and efficient architecture.
