items.title: Creating a Secure Deployment Pipeline for AWS Lambda Using Bitbucket Pipelines
items.author: Carlos Biagolini-Jr.
items.content:
This tutorial provides a step-by-step guide to creating a secure and automated CI/CD pipeline for deploying AWS Lambda functions using . The pipeline leverages an IAM Role with to grant Bitbucket Pipelines permission to deploy code to AWS without using long-term credentials.

You will need an AWS account with permissions to manage IAM roles and Lambda functions. Additionally, ensure that your Bitbucket repository contains the Lambda function code. Familiarity with AWS IAM, Lambda, and Bitbucket Pipelines is recommended.

Step 1: Find your OpenID Connect in Bitbucket.

Ger your repository OpenID Connect to securely communicate with AWS IAM. Access Repository settings, navigate to OpenID Connect, Note down the contents of Identity provider URL and Audience to use in the next step. Data will looks like:

  • : https://api.bitbucket.org/2.0/workspaces/<your-workspace>/pipelines-config/identity/oidc
  • : ari:cloud:bitbucket::workspace/xxxxx-xxxxxxxx-xxxxxxxx-xxxxxxxxx

Step 2: Create an IAM Identity Provider for Bitbucket

In the AWS Console, go to and click on .

Configure the provider as follows:

  • : OpenID Connect
  • : https://api.bitbucket.org/2.0/workspaces/<your-workspace>/pipelines-config/identity/oidc
  • : ari:cloud:bitbucket::workspace/xxxxx-xxxxxxxx-xxxxxxxx-xxxxxxxxx
Use data from Step 1 on ‘Identity provider URL’ and ‘Audience’

This configuration allows Bitbucket Pipelines to securely authenticate to AWS using temporary credentials via OIDC.

Step 3: Create an IAM Role for Lambda Deployment

Navigate to , and choose .

  • : Web identity
  • : Select the Bitbucket OIDC provider created in Step 1
  • : sts.amazonaws.com

Then configure the trust policy to only allow requests from your Bitbucket repository. Here is an example trust relationship:

{    "Version": "2012-10-17",        {            "Principal": {
                "Federated": "arn:aws:iam::<account-id>:oidc-provider/api.bitbucket.org/2.0/workspaces/<your-workspace>/pipelines-config/identity/oidc"
            },
            "Action": "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity",
            "Condition": {                    "api.bitbucket.org/2.0/workspaces/<your-workspace>/pipelines-config/identity/oidc:aud": "ari:cloud:bitbucket::workspace/xxxxx-xxxxxxxx-xxxxxxxx-xxxxxxxxx"            }    ]

Create the role with a name like bitbucket-pipelines-lambda-deployment. Attach the following inline policy:

{  "Version": "2012-10-17",    {      "Action": [
        "lambda:UpdateFunctionCode"
      ],
      "Resource": "arn:aws:lambda:<region>:<account-id>:function:<function-name>"
    }}

Step 4: Set Bitbucket Repository Variables

Go to your Bitbucket repository settings and open under . Add the following variables:

  • AWS_ROLE_ARN — IAM role ARN created in step 3
  • AWS_REGION — Region where your Lambda is deployed (e.g., us-east-1)
  • AWS_LAMBDA_NAME — The name of your Lambda function

Step 5: Define Bitbucket Pipeline Configuration

Create or update the file bitbucket-pipelines.yml in the root of your repository with the following configuration:

image: amazon/aws-cli  branches:      - step:
          name: Build and Deploy Lambda Function
          services:          oidc: true # Enable authentication via OIDC            # Unset previous credential environment variables
            - unset AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID
            - unset AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY
            - unset AWS_SESSION_TOKEN
            # Check the operating system
            - echo "Checking OS distribution"
            # Install the zip package (assuming a Red Hat-based distribution)
            - yum install -y zip
            # Set up OIDC authentication for AWS
            - export AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE=$(pwd)/web-identity-token
            - echo $BITBUCKET_STEP_OIDC_TOKEN > $(pwd)/web-identity-token
            - aws sts get-caller-identity # Confirm that the credentials are correct
            # Create a zip file with the Lambda function source code
            - echo "Zipping Lambda function code"
            # Update the Lambda function code with the generated zip file
            - echo "Deploying Lambda function"
            - aws lambda update-function-code --function-name ${AWS_LAMBDA_NAME} --zip-file fileb://function.zip --region ${AWS_REGION}

Step 6: Trigger the Deployment

Commit and push changes to the main branch. This will trigger the Bitbucket Pipeline and deploy your Lambda function securely using temporary OIDC credentials.

You’ve successfully built a secure CI/CD pipeline using , , and . This method improves your security posture by avoiding static credentials and enabling seamless automation.

For more content on , , and , connect with me:

Happy automating with ! 🚀

items.title: How to Build a CI/CD Pipeline Using Jenkins
items.author: Omi Sneha
items.content:
By Omi Sneha —May 5, 2025

Jenkins enables automation of the software development process through CI/CD (Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment), helping teams to build, test, and release applications more efficiently. It simplifies workflows, maintains code quality, and minimizes the need for manual effort.

1. What is CI/CD? 3. Understanding Jenkins Pipelines

4. Benefits of Using Jenkins for CI/CD

5. Step-by-Step Guide to Building a CI/CD Pipeline with Jenkins

6. Challenges and Solutions in Jenkins CI/CD Pipelines

7. Best Practices for CI/CD Using Jenkins

, short for and , refers to a set of practices that streamline and automate the software development lifecycle. These approaches are widely adopted in modern development to enhance speed, collaboration, and code reliability.

Continuous Integration (CI)

Continuous Integration is the practice of regularly merging code changes from multiple contributors into a shared codebase. Each integration is automatically tested, allowing developers to catch bugs and conflicts early in the process.

By integrating frequently — often multiple times a day — teams can identify issues before they escalate, maintain a stable codebase, and ensure higher code quality throughout the development cycle.

Continuous Deployment (CD)

Continuous Deployment takes things a step further by automating the release of validated code to production environments. Once changes pass all automated tests in the CI phase, they are immediately deployed to users — without manual approval steps.

This results in faster delivery of new features and bug fixes, improved responsiveness to user needs, and a reduction in deployment errors caused by manual steps.

is a widely-used, open-source automation server designed to manage and streamline different stages of the software development lifecycle — including building, testing, and deploying applications. With its extensive library of plugins, Jenkins supports robust CI/CD pipelines, enabling development teams to automate repetitive tasks and maintain code consistency.

By integrating seamlessly with a wide range of development tools, Jenkins boosts collaboration among teams, ensures high-quality code, and accelerates the release of software updates.

Major tech companies like , , and utilize Jenkins as a core part of their CI/CD infrastructure to deliver software faster and more reliably.

Understanding Jenkins Pipeline

A is a powerful set of features that allows developers to automate the entire software delivery process — such as building, testing, and deploying — by defining each stage in a structured sequence. It enables teams to represent their CI/CD workflows as code, which can be stored in version control systems, making them easy to track, update, and reuse.

Jenkins Pipelines come in two styles —  and  — offering flexibility to suit different project needs and coding preferences.

Benefits of Using Jenkins for CI/CD

Jenkins offers several essential benefits for automating CI/CD workflows, making it a key tool in modern software development:

  • Automation of Repetitive TasksJenkins automates the build, test, and deployment processes, reducing manual errors and saving valuable time.
  • Integration with Multiple ToolsWith a wide range of plugins, Jenkins can easily integrate with version control systems, testing tools, and deployment platforms, creating a streamlined workflow.
  • Jenkins can scale to meet the needs of both small teams and large enterprises, supporting distributed builds and multiple agents to optimize performance.
  • Jenkins automates tests and deployments, helping teams detect issues early, which accelerates the development cycle and improves product quality.
  • Extensive Community SupportWith a large and active community, Jenkins provides a wealth of resources, including detailed documentation, plugins, and solutions to tackle various CI/CD challenges.

Step-by-Step Guide to Building a CI/CD Pipeline with Jenkins

Before creating a CI/CD pipeline with Jenkins, make sure to complete the following prerequisites:

  1. Download and install Jenkins from the official Jenkins website. Alternatively, you can use Docker to install Jenkins by running this command:
docker run -d \  --name jenkins \
  -p 8080:8080 -p 50000:50000 \
  -v jenkins_home:/var/jenkins_home \

2. Configure Jenkins and add necessary plugins

Setting up Jenkins involves selecting the necessary plugins for the project. Commonly used options at the start include the Git plugin and the Pipeline plugin, which support version control and CI/CD processes.

Login to Jenkins and click on “New Item.”

Select the “Pipeline” option from the menu, provide a name for the pipeline, and click “OK.”

5. Configure the pipeline

The CI/CD pipeline can be set up through the pipeline configuration interface, where build triggers and various settings are defined. The key part is the ‘Pipeline Definition’ area, where pipeline stages are specified. Jenkins allows the use of both declarative and scripted formats for defining these stages.

(For additional details, see the official Jenkins documentation.)

Here’s a basic example of a ‘Hello World’ pipeline script:

pipeline {    agent any        stage('Install Dependencies') {                sh 'echo "Installing dependencies..."'        }            steps {
                sh 'echo "Building the project..."'
            }            steps {
                sh 'echo "Running tests..."'
                sh 'echo "All tests passed successfully!"'        }            steps {
                sh 'echo "Deployment successful!"'
            }    }

Select Apply and then Save to complete the setup of a basic pipeline.

Select ‘Build Now’ to initiate the pipeline run.

This action will trigger the execution of the pipeline stages, with the outcome shown in the ‘Builds’ section. The pipeline is now built successfully:

To confirm that the pipeline ran successfully, review the console output of the build process.

The console log reflects a successful build completion status.

7. Expanding the Pipeline

The pipeline doesn’t have to stop at basic build and deploy steps. It can be extended to include multiple stages that reflect a real-world workflow — like initial checks, setup, testing, and more granular deployment steps. When new stages are added, Jenkins updates the visual stage view accordingly, making it easy to track the execution flow. Each stage contributes clarity and structure, which becomes especially helpful in large projects.

8. Visualizing the Pipeline

To better understand how the pipeline progresses over time, Jenkins provides visualization tools. With the help of plugins like the Pipeline Timeline, it’s possible to view a chronological breakdown of all pipeline events. This visualization gives teams a clearer picture of execution duration, bottlenecks, and overall efficiency.

This completes a basic CI/CD pipeline setup using Jenkins, with a flexible foundation that can grow to include version control, test automation, and robust deployment practices.

Integrating Git and Docker with Jenkins

A key advantage of Jenkins as a CI/CD tool lies in its seamless integration with version control systems such as . Git serves as the foundation for modern source code management, enabling teams to track changes, manage branches, and collaborate effectively. When connected to Jenkins, Git repositories become the starting point for automated build and deployment workflows.

Every time a commit is pushed to a Git repository — hosted on platforms like , , or  — Jenkins can be configured to automatically detect these changes. It then pulls the updated code, initiates a build pipeline, runs tests, and prepares the application for deployment. This continuous integration process ensures rapid feedback, code reliability, and reduced risk of integration conflicts.

Incorporating into this workflow further enhances the pipeline’s efficiency and consistency. Jenkins can build Docker images directly from the version-controlled code, run tests in containerized environments, and push final images to container registries. This containerization approach standardizes environments across development, testing, and production, eliminating discrepancies and deployment issues.

The integration flow is visually represented in the following image:

By combining Git, Jenkins, and Docker, CI/CD pipelines become more robust, scalable, and aligned with modern DevOps practices.

Challenges and Solutions in Jenkins CI/CD Pipelines

While Jenkins is a pivotal tool for CI/CD, certain obstacles can affect its efficiency. By addressing these challenges, teams can ensure smoother pipeline execution and enhanced management.

Challenge: Unexpected errors in the pipeline can result in failed builds or tests.: Integrate comprehensive error handling and automatic retry mechanisms to mitigate transient issues.

Challenge: Jenkins may struggle with scaling to handle increased workloads or growing projects.: Utilize Jenkins agents, distributed builds, and parallel execution to improve scalability and resource management.

Challenge: Complex pipelines with many steps may lead to prolonged build times.: Optimize the pipeline by running tests in parallel and splitting large tasks into smaller, more manageable jobs.

Challenge: Maintaining complex configurations across multiple teams and projects can be challenging.: Standardize configurations using shared libraries, templates, and configuration as code for consistency across projects.

Challenge: Exposing sensitive information or enabling unauthorized access to Jenkins can result in security vulnerabilities.: Implement robust role-based access control (RBAC), and use secure credential management practices to safeguard sensitive data.

6. Integration with Third-Party Tools

Challenge Integrating Jenkins with third-party tools (like version control, testing tools, and deployment platforms) may sometimes be tricky or error-prone.: Leverage Jenkins plugins, ensure proper configurations, and keep integration documentation updated for seamless connectivity.

Best Practices for CI/CD with Jenkins

Adopting best practices for Jenkins CI/CD pipelines optimizes workflows, minimizes errors, and improves overall efficiency.

  • Automate the Entire Process: Automate all stages of the build, testing, and deployment pipeline to minimize manual intervention and enhance pipeline consistency.
  • Leverage Declarative Pipelines: Declarative pipelines simplify Jenkins file management by clearly defining stages, steps, and conditions, making them easier to read, maintain, and scale.
  • Improve build efficiency by executing tasks such as tests in parallel, maximizing resource use and reducing build times.
  • Implement Version Control: Store Jenkins files alongside the application code in version control to ensure up-to-date and manageable pipeline configurations.
  • Monitor and Track Builds: Continuously monitor build statuses and maintain comprehensive logs to swiftly detect and resolve issues, ensuring smooth pipeline operations.”

CI/CD Pipelines Streamline Workflow and Improve Quality

An effectively set up CI/CD pipeline significantly reduces manual intervention, boosting the efficiency and accuracy of software delivery. By automating repetitive tasks and minimizing human error, the delivery process becomes faster and more reliable. This automation not only improves product quality but also accelerates the development cycle — benefiting developers, end-users, and the business alike.

Organizations like Humana have seen measurable improvements in velocity, quality, and operational efficiency by adopting modern development environments that support CI/CD practices for editing and debugging code.

In conclusion, Jenkins is an essential tool for automating CI/CD pipelines, providing fast, reliable, and consistent testing processes. When integrated with BrowserStack for real-device testing, teams can significantly elevate their testing strategies, ensuring that applications perform optimally across real browsers and devices. This combination not only streamlines the development lifecycle but also enhances the overall quality of the software.

items.title: MCP Server Hands-on for DevOps Engineers(1) Setting up environment
items.author: Shota Takano
items.content:
This is the first in a series of blog posts exploring developing and using MCP server in your WSL2/VSCode with github copilot environment.

This article series shows..

the following architecture in the picture below. I will show you how to develop MCP Server to call api endpoints of zabbix server.

  • DevOps Engineers who want to automate their operations using LLM.
  • Engineers who want to know how to develop MCP Server under WSL2 environment.

setting up environments to develop MCP Server.

  1. Installing Zabbix Server and getting API Key from Zabbix Server

2. Installing uv package manager

3. Call API from python script

  • WSL2(Ubuntu) environment with docker/docker-composeThis article is tested under Ubuntu22.04
  • VSCode with github copilotCheck out if Agent mode is enabled in your copilot chat window.

Step1 Install Zabbix Server

You can install zabbix server 7.2 as follows.Details are written in the official documents.

$ git clone https://github.com/zabbix/zabbix-docker.git$ git checkout -b 7.2$ docker compose -f ./docker-compose_v3_ubuntu_pgsql_latest.yaml up -d

If you change port, change zabbix-docker/.env file(L49–50).

ZABBIX_WEB_NGINX_HTTP_PORT=xxxx

Then, if all containers remains up, log in to the server http://localhost:portPort = 80 or changed port by you.

Usename = Admin Password = zabbix

In the left columnm, click User settings -> API Tokens.

Next, click  button.

Copy the Auth token(=API Token).

Step2 Installing uv package manager

$ curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

Then creating the project for this article.Confirming main.py says hello.

$ uv init --app zabbix-mcp$ cd zabbix-mcpUsing CPython 3.12.7 interpreter at: xxx
Creating virtual environment at: .venv
Hello from zabbix-mcp!

Step3 Call API from python script

In the last step, you will call Zabbix API and get the list of watched hosts. By default, host is Zabbix server itself.

To call API from VSCode, get the IPv4 address by following comannd.Note: The address is temporal, so it is not suitable for production environment.

$ ip -4 addr show dev eth0 | grep -oP '(?<=inet\s)\d+(\.\d+){3}' | head -n1172.xx.xx.xx

After that, setting environment variables.

$ export ZABBIX_URL=http://172.xx.xxx.xx:xxx/api_jsonrpc.php$ export API_TOKEN=xxxxx

Now you can call get host by running the script by “’’.

import osimport requests
# Retrieve Zabbix URL and API token from environment variables
ZABBIX_URL = os.getenv("ZABBIX_URL")
API_TOKEN = os.getenv("API_TOKEN")
    # Set up headers for the API request        "Content-Type": "application/json",
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {API_TOKEN}"
    }
    # Define the payload for the Zabbix API request
    payload = {        "method": "host.get",            "output": ["hostid", "host", "name"]  # Specify the fields to retrieve        "id": 1  # Unique identifier for the request
    # Send the API request to the Zabbix server
    response = requests.post(ZABBIX_URL, headers=headers, json=payload)
    # Check if the HTTP response status code indicates an error
    if response.status_code != 200:
        print(f"HTTP error: {response.status_code}")
        print(response.text)  # Print the error details
        exit(1)  # Exit the script with an error code
    # Parse the JSON response from the API
    data = response.json()
    # Check if the response contains an error from the Zabbix API
    if "error" in data:
        print("Zabbix API error:")
        print(data["error"])  # Print the error details
        exit(1)  # Exit the script with an error code
    # Iterate through the list of hosts in the response and print their details
    for host in data["result"]:
        print(f"{host['hostid']}: {host['host']} ({host['name']})")
if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()  # Execute the main function

Successful out put is like as follows. (The number is depends on your environment.)

$ uv run pre_test.py 10084: Zabbix server (Zabbix server)

items.title: Zero Downtime Deployment for Laravel with GitHub Actions
items.author: Dominik
items.content:
What is Zero Downtime Deployment?Zero downtime deployment means updating your app without users noticing any interruption — the site stays online and responsive while the new version is being deployed.

0. Create User (optional — you can use your non-root user)

# Create user with proper primary groupsudo adduser deployer --ingroup www-data
sudo usermod -aG sudo deployer
echo "deployer ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL" | sudo tee /etc/sudoers.d/deployer
echo 'Defaults:deployer !requiretty' | sudo tee -a /etc/sudoers.d/deployer
# Fix home directory permissions
sudo chmod 711 /home/deployer
# Update the systemapt update
sudo apt install -y nginx php-fpm mariadb-server ufw fail2ban acl
sudo apt install -y php8.3-{cli,common,curl,xml,mbstring,zip,mysql,gd,intl,bcmath,redis,imagick,opcache,tokenizer,dom,fileinfo}
sudo systemctl restart php8.3-fpm

Create a new Nginx configuration file:

sudo nano /etc/nginx/sites-available/laravel

Add the following configuration:

server {    listen 80;    server_name your-domain.com;
    root /home/deployer/laravel/current/public;
    add_header X-Frame-Options "SAMEORIGIN";
    add_header X-Content-Type-Options "nosniff";
    add_header X-XSS-Protection "1; mode=block";
    add_header Referrer-Policy "strict-origin-when-cross-origin";
    add_header Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains" always;
    add_header Permissions-Policy "geolocation=(), midi=(), sync-xhr=(), microphone=(), camera=(), magnetometer=(), gyroscope=(), fullscreen=(self), payment=()";    charset utf-8;    tcp_nopush on;    keepalive_timeout 65;
    client_max_body_size 100M;
    location ~* \.(jpg|jpeg|png|gif|ico|css|js|svg|woff|woff2|ttf|eot|webp)$ {
        expires 1y;
        add_header Cache-Control "public, immutable";
        access_log off;        try_files $uri =404;        try_files $uri $uri/ /index.php?$query_string;
    location = /favicon.ico { access_log off; log_not_found off; }
    location = /robots.txt  { access_log off; log_not_found off; }
    error_page 404 /index.php;
    location ~ ^/index\.php(/|$) {
        fastcgi_pass unix:/var/run/php/php8.3-fpm.sock;
        fastcgi_param SCRIPT_FILENAME $realpath_root$fastcgi_script_name;
        include fastcgi_params;
        fastcgi_buffer_size 128k;
        fastcgi_buffers 4 256k;
        fastcgi_busy_buffers_size 256k;
        fastcgi_read_timeout 300;
        fastcgi_hide_header X-Powered-By;
    }
    location ~ /\.(?!well-known).* {
        deny all;        log_not_found off;    gzip_vary on;    gzip_comp_level 6;
    gzip_types text/plain text/css text/xml application/json application/javascript application/xml+rss application/atom+xml image/svg+xml;
    gzip_min_length 1024;    gzip_disable "MSIE [1-6]\.";
sudo ln -s /etc/nginx/sites-available/laravel /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/sudo nginx -t
sudo systemctl restart nginx

3. Update PHP-FPM Configuration

# Runnano /etc/php/8.3/fpm/pool.d/www.conf

Replace it with the following configuration and restart PHP-FPM:

[www]user = deployer
listen = /var/run/php/php8.3-fpm.sock
listen.owner = www-datalisten.mode = 0660pm.max_children = 20pm.min_spare_servers = 2pm.process_idle_timeout = 10s
php_admin_value[open_basedir] = /home/deployer/laravel/current/:/home/deployer/laravel/releases/:/home/deployer/laravel/shared/:/tmp/:/var/lib/php/sessions/
php_admin_value[disable_functions] = "exec,passthru,shell_exec,system,proc_open,popen"
php_admin_flag[expose_php] = off
php_admin_value[memory_limit] = 256M
php_admin_value[max_execution_time] = 120
php_admin_value[realpath_cache_size] = 4096K
php_admin_value[realpath_cache_ttl] = 600
php_admin_value[opcache.enable] = 1
php_admin_value[opcache.memory_consumption] = 128

4. Update PHP Configuration

# Runnano /etc/php/8.3/fpm/php.ini

Replace it with the following configuration:

[PHP]expose_php = Offmax_input_time = 60error_reporting = E_ALL & ~E_DEPRECATED & ~E_STRICTdisplay_startup_errors = Offerror_log = /var/log/php8.3-fpm.logopcache.memory_consumption=256
opcache.interned_strings_buffer=32
opcache.max_accelerated_files=20000
opcache.validate_timestamps=0
opcache.enable_cli=0
opcache.jit_buffer_size=256M
opcache.jit=1235
realpath_cache_size=4096K
realpath_cache_ttl=600session.gc_divisor=100
session.gc_maxlifetime=1440
session.save_handler = redis
session.save_path = "tcp://127.0.0.1:6379"
upload_max_filesize = 64M
post_max_size = 64Mrequest_order = "GP"date.timezone = Europe/Warsaw

5. Set Up Directory Structure

# Create structure with proper permissionssudo mkdir -p /home/deployer/laravel/{releases,shared}
sudo chown -R deployer:www-data /home/deployer/laravel
sudo chmod -R 2775 /home/deployer/laravelsudo mkdir -p /home/deployer/laravel/shared/storage/{app,framework,logs}
sudo mkdir -p /home/deployer/laravel/shared/storage/framework/{cache,sessions,views}
sudo chmod -R 775 /home/deployer/laravel/shared
# Set ACL for future files
sudo setfacl -Rdm g:www-data:rwx /home/deployer/laravel

6. Set Up SSH Key for GitHub Actions (as deployer user)

# Create SSH directorymkdir -p ~/.sshssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 -C "github-actions-deploy"
# Add public key to authorized_keys
cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
# Display the private key

Add the following secrets to your GitHub repository:

  • SSH_HOST: Your VPS IP address or domain
  • SSH_USER: Your VPS username
  • SSH_KEY: The private SSH key generated above
  • SSH_PORT: The SSH port (default is 22)

Add variable for .env production file

  • ENV_FILE: The contents of your .env file

8. Set Up SSL with Let’s Encrypt (Optional but Recommended)

# Install Certbotsudo apt install -y certbot python3-certbot-nginxsudo certbot --nginx -d your-domain.comsudo systemctl status certbot.timer

In main directory create script deploy.sh

#!/bin/bashset -o pipefailAPP_GROUP="www-data"
APP_BASE="/home/$APP_USER/laravel"
RELEASES_DIR="$APP_BASE/releases"
SHARED_DIR="$APP_BASE/shared"
CURRENT_LINK="$APP_BASE/current"
NOW=$(date +%Y-%m-%d-%H%M%S)-$(openssl rand -hex 3)
RELEASE_DIR="$RELEASES_DIR/$NOW"
ARCHIVE_NAME="release.tar.gz"
echo "▶️ Create directories..."
mkdir -p "$RELEASES_DIR" "$SHARED_DIR/storage" "$SHARED_DIR/bootstrap_cache"
mkdir -p "$SHARED_DIR/storage/framework/views"
mkdir -p "$SHARED_DIR/storage/framework/cache"
mkdir -p "$SHARED_DIR/storage/framework/sessions"
mkdir -p "$SHARED_DIR/storage/logs"
echo "▶️ Unpacking release..."
mkdir "$RELEASE_DIR"
tar -xzf "$APP_BASE/$ARCHIVE_NAME" -C "$RELEASE_DIR"
rm "$APP_BASE/$ARCHIVE_NAME"
echo "▶️ Symlink storage..."
rm -rf "$RELEASE_DIR/storage"
ln -s "$SHARED_DIR/storage" "$RELEASE_DIR/storage"
rm -rf "$RELEASE_DIR/bootstrap/cache"
ln -s "$SHARED_DIR/bootstrap_cache" "$RELEASE_DIR/bootstrap/cache"
ln -sf "$SHARED_DIR/.env" "$RELEASE_DIR/.env"
echo "▶️ Optimizing application..."
cd "$RELEASE_DIR"
php artisan optimize:clear
php artisan optimize
echo "▶️ Migrating database..."
php artisan migrate --force
echo "▶️ Symlink current..."
ln -sfn "$RELEASE_DIR" "$CURRENT_LINK"
echo "▶️ Cleaning old releases..."
cd "$RELEASES_DIR"
ls -dt */ | tail -n +6 | xargs -r rm -rf
echo "✅ Deploy finished: $NOW"

And .github/workflows/workflow.yaml

name: Zero Downtime Deployment  push:      - main  test:    runs-on: ubuntu-latest      mysql:        env:
          MYSQL_DATABASE: laravel_test
          MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD: secret          - 3306:3306          --health-cmd="mysqladmin ping --silent"          --health-timeout=2s    steps:        uses: actions/checkout@v4        uses: shivammathur/setup-php@v2          php-version: '8.4'
          extensions: mbstring, dom, fileinfo, mysql, zip, gd, intl, redis, imagick
          coverage: xdebug        uses: actions/setup-node@v4          node-version: '22'
      - name: Copy .env.testing
        run: cp .env.testing .env
      - name: Get Composer cache directory
        id: composer-cache
        run: echo "dir=$(composer config cache-files-dir)" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
      - name: Cache Composer dependencies
        uses: actions/cache@v4          path: |            ${{ steps.composer-cache.outputs.dir }}
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-composer-${{ hashFiles('**/composer.lock') }}
          restore-keys: ${{ runner.os }}-composer-
      - name: Install Composer dependencies
        run: composer install --prefer-dist --no-progress
      - name: Install NPM dependencies
        run: npm ci        run: npm run build
      - name: Run code quality checks
        run: |          composer pint          npm run types
      - name: Run tests (with Pest)
        env:          DB_HOST: 127.0.0.1          DB_DATABASE: laravel_test          DB_PASSWORD: secret          REDIS_PORT: 6379        run: ./vendor/bin/pest    name: 🏗️ Build Release    runs-on: ubuntu-latest      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4          node-version: '22'
      - name: Install NPM dependencies
        run: npm ci        run: npm run build        uses: shivammathur/setup-php@v2          php-version: '8.4'
          extensions: mbstring, dom, fileinfo, mysql, zip, gd, intl, redis, imagick
          tools: composer:v2
      - name: Install Composer dependencies
        run: composer install --no-dev --prefer-dist --no-interaction --no-progress
      - name: Create release archive
        run: |
          # Utwórz folder tymczasowy o nazwie "release" i skopiuj do niego projekt bez niepotrzebnych katalogów
          mkdir release          cp -r !(release|.git|tests|node_modules|release.tar.gz) release/
          tar -czf release.tar.gz -C release .
          rm -rf release
      - name: Upload release artifact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4          name: release    name: 🚀 Deploy to Server    runs-on: ubuntu-latest      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
        uses: webfactory/ssh-agent@v0.9.1          ssh-private-key: ${{ secrets.SSH_KEY }}
      - name: Setup known_hosts
        run: |          ssh-keyscan -p ${{ secrets.SSH_PORT }} ${{ secrets.SSH_HOST }} >> ~/.ssh/known_hosts
      - name: Download release artifact
        uses: actions/download-artifact@v4          name: release
      - name: Create .env file from GitHub Variables
        run: |
          echo "${{ vars.ENV_FILE }}" > .env
      - name: Upload release to server
        run: |
          scp -vvv -P ${{ secrets.SSH_PORT }} release.tar.gz ${{ secrets.SSH_USER }}@${{ secrets.SSH_HOST }}:/home/${{ secrets.SSH_USER }}/laravel/
      - name: Upload .env file to shared directory
        run: |
          scp -P ${{ secrets.SSH_PORT }} .env ${{ secrets.SSH_USER }}@${{ secrets.SSH_HOST }}:/home/${{ secrets.SSH_USER }}/laravel/shared/.env
      - name: Run deploy script on server
        run: |
          ssh -p ${{ secrets.SSH_PORT }} ${{ secrets.SSH_USER }}@${{ secrets.SSH_HOST }} 'bash -s' < ./deploy.sh
  1. Push your code to the main branch to trigger the deployment.
  2. Monitor the GitHub Actions workflow to ensure it completes successfully.
  3. Check your website to verify the deployment.
  • : Ensure all directories have the correct ownership and permissions.
  • : Check the Nginx error logs with sudo tail -f /var/log/nginx/error.log.
  • : Check the PHP-FPM error logs with sudo tail -f /var/log/php8.3-fpm.log.
  • : Check the GitHub Actions logs for detailed error messages.

This guide provides a comprehensive setup for deploying Laravel applications on a VPS with zero downtime. By following these steps, you can ensure a smooth and efficient deployment process.

items.title: Debugging MongoDB PHP Extension Errors in Docker: The Silent Cost of Upgrading
items.author: Noelhhh
items.content:
Recently, while maintaining a Laravel application using the jenssegers/laravel-mongodb package, I encountered multiple unexpected runtime errors after upgrading our Docker base image since mid April 2025:

"Undefined constant MongoDB\Driver\ReadPreference::RP_PRIMARY"
“Expected integer or object, string given”

Both of these errors seemed unrelated at first glance — one was a missing constant, and the other was a type error when inserting dates. But the real issue? 👉 A silent upgrade of the MongoDB PHP extension from version to .

The PHP MongoDB extension (ext-mongodb) powers low-level interactions with MongoDB in PHP applications. Many Laravel projects use it indirectly via packages like jenssegers/laravel-mongodb.

  • Version exposes constants like RP_PRIMARY and accepts DateTime or string coercion for UTCDateTime.
  • Version is stricter, dropping some constants and enforcing (e.g., you must pass an integer or a DateTimeInterface, not a string).

If your Docker image installs the latest extension by default (which it likely does if you use pecl install mongodb), you’ll get  — and your legacy Laravel app will break.

Step 0: Check Your Current MongoDB Extension Version

Before making any changes, it’s good to confirm which version is currently installed inside your running container:

docker exec -it your-container-name php -i | grep -i mongo
MongoDB support => enabledMongoDB version => 2.0.0

If you see 2.x.x and your code expects 1.x.x, you’ve found your problem.

Step 1: Lock the Extension Version

In your Dockerfile, update the install command to specify a compatible version:

RUN pecl install mongodb-1.20.1 \    && docker-php-ext-enable mongodb
1.20.1 to match the latest stable 1.x version that works with your PHP environment.

Step 2: Rebuild the Container

docker-compose builddocker-compose up -d

If you still see the wrong version, try removing the old container:

docker-compose down -vdocker-compose up --build -d

Step 3: Confirm the Version After Rebuild

docker exec -it your-container-name php -i | grep -i mongodb
MongoDB support => enabledMongoDB version => 1.20.1
  • : The 1.x MongoDB extension the latest PHP versions. If you’re on PHP 8.3+, you might need to update your MongoDB libraries to work with extension 2.x.
  • Always pin your PECL extension versions in Docker — especially when working with legacy code or third-party packages that assume older behaviors.
  • Check your Composer dependency tree for libraries that implicitly depend on ext-mongodb and may break under stricter typing.

This kind of breakage often flies under the radar — until runtime. Version pinning in Docker isn’t just about repeatable builds; it’s about protecting your application from invisible upgrades that can cripple production code.

If you see MongoDB-related exceptions suddenly appear after a rebuild, don’t just debug the line — check the extension version first.

items.title: Event-Driven Architecture and RabbitMQ
items.author: Raghavendra Raikar
items.content:
Introduction: The Power of Event-Driven Architecture

Why Event-Driven Architecture?

Traditionally, in software applications, services communicate with each other through API calls where one service requests data or sends instructions to another service and waits for a response. This method of communication works perfectly in simple small scale applications but can become quite problematic when dealing with larger scale applications and lead to scalability issues, failures, and loss of requests. Let’s take a look at the drawbacks:

1. Blocking and Performance Bottlenecks

When one service waits on another for a response, it creates , leading to:

  • if the dependent service is slow.
  • if the service goes down, causing .
  • An e-commerce calls the to verify stock. If the inventory service is , the checkout process , delaying transactions.

2. System-Wide Failures Due to Single Points of Failure

A failure in one service can trigger a across dependent services.

  • In a , if the fails, doctors can’t access histories, pharmacies can’t verify prescriptions, and , delaying treatments and payments — even though other services remain operational.

3. Request Loss and Poor User Experience

If a service fails during high traffic, in-flight requests may be , leading to:

    • A is submitted, but the payment service crashes mid-transaction. With no retry mechanism, the transaction is , frustrating users.

    Synchronous API calls force all services to scale together, leading to:

    • , while others remain .
    • , limiting growth.
    • A ride-sharing app’s calls the for driver locations. If request trips simultaneously, the GPS service , slowing down the entire system.

    Modern software applications must be scalable, resilient, and loosely coupled. One of the best ways to achieve these goals is through Event-Driven Architecture (EDA). Instead of direct communication between services, events trigger responses asynchronously, making systems more scalable, responsive, and fault-tolerant.

    Key Benefits of EDA:1. Decoupling: Services interact .2. Asynchronous event processing prevents system overload.3. Real-time Responsiveness: React to user actions or system events instantly.4. Services function independently — even if some fail, the system continues operating.

    How Does a Message Broker Fit In?

    In event-driven systems, services must communicate effectively, even if they don’t know about each other directly. This is where come into play.

    What is a Message Broker?

    A acts as a middle man that enables asynchronous communication between services. This ensures decoupling and fault tolerance.

    • Instead of services , they send and receive messages via a .
    • The broker stores, routes, and delivers messages, ensuring they reach the right consumers.
    • : Messages are queued and processed independently.
    • : Ensures messages are received and acknowledged.
    • : Can distribute messages across multiple consumers.
    • : Producers and consumers don’t need to know each other.

    Understanding Producer-Consumer Model

    In a messaging system like RabbitMQ, two key roles exist: and .

    A is anything that creates and sends messages to a message broker (like RabbitMQ). It who will receive the messages — it just .

    • A user on an e-commerce site.
    • The sends an to RabbitMQ.

    A is anything that receives and processes messages from the message broker. It listens for specific messages and takes action when they arrive.

    • The Payment Service (consumer) listens for .
    • When it detects a new order, it automatically.

    Instead of services directly calling each other, they communicate through RabbitMQ, making the system faster, more flexible, and fault-tolerant.

    RabbitMQ: A Leading Message Broker

    RabbitMQ is a widely used open-source message broker that enables asynchronous communication between services, ensuring high reliability, flexible message routing, and guaranteed delivery. It implements the Advanced Message Queuing Protocol (AMQP), making it ideal for event-driven architectures, task queues, and real-time applications.

    Reliable Message Delivery — Uses acknowledgments and persistence to prevent message loss. — Supports (Direct, Topic, Fanout, Headers) to control message flow.Scalability & Fault Tolerance — Allows multiple consumers to process messages independently, preventing bottlenecks.

    How RabbitMQ Works: AMQP and Message Exchanges

    RabbitMQ does not send messages directly from producer to consumer. Instead, it follows the AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol) model, where messages pass through an , which determines how they are routed to queues.

    Message Flow in RabbitMQ:

    1. : Sends messages to an instead of a specific queue.
    2. : Determines based on its type.⃣
    3. : Stores messages until consumers are ready to process them.
    4. : Listens to the queue and processes messages asynchronously.

    Message Exchange Types in RabbitMQ

    RabbitMQ provides , allowing for different message distribution strategies.

    Core Concepts of RabbitMQ

    Producer (Message Publisher)

    A is responsible for generating and sending messages to an exchange. It who will receive the messages — its job is to publish them.

    • A user on an e-commerce site.
    • The sends an “order.placed” event to RabbitMQ.
    • Uses a for .
    • The (order.placed) ensures the message reaches the correct consumers.

    Consumer (Message Subscriber)

    A listens for and processes them .

    • The Payment Service (consumer) listens for events.
    • When it detects a new order, it automatically.
    • The consumer to “order.placed” events.
    • Multiple consumers (e.g., Inventory, Notifications, Logging) can react without affecting each other.

    Final Thoughts: Why RabbitMQ for Event-Driven Architectures?

    Messages are acknowledged before removal. Multiple exchange types for advanced routing. Load balancing across multiple instances. Durable queues, clustering, and retry mechanisms.Asynchronous Communication: Perfect for event-driven microservices.

items.title: Top 7 Logical Database Design Tools in 2025
items.author: Dbschema Pro
items.content:
Before the first line of SQL is written, before indexes are added or columns optimized, every serious database begins with a solid logical design. It’s the blueprint phase, where you define the what, not the how. And in 2025, data professionals will have some remarkable tools at their disposal to craft smart, scalable, and well-documented logical models.

But not all tools are really made for logical design. Some just let you draw diagrams, and others focus too much on the physical side of things.

So let’s keep it simple and focus on what actually matters. Here are the top tools in 2025 that let you do think entities, relationships, naming standards, metadata, and documentation, before you actually build or deploy anything to a real database.

ERwin has been in the data modeling game for decades, and it shows. It doesn’t just support logical modeling-it in it.

  • Dedicated layers for conceptual, logical, and physical models
  • Full support for relationship cardinalities, inheritance, and business rules
  • Robust naming standards and domain type management
  • Glossary and metadata dictionary
  • Logical-to-physical model transformations

: Desktop application (Windows): Paid license; free trial available

: Enterprise data teams, regulated industries, complex metadata governance.

Personal note: Very powerful tool, especially for big companies, but it can feel a bit heavy if you’re just starting out. Also, it’s one of the most expensive tools on the list.

DbSchema has quietly carved out a niche as a go-to tool for logical modeling with a visual-first, user-friendly approach.

  • Visual design of database-independent models
  • Define relationship types (1:1, 1:N, N:M)
  • Apply naming conventions and data type abstraction
  • Multiple layout views for organizing large diagrams
  • Native Git integration for version control
  • Export to interactive HTML5 documentation

: Desktop application (cross-platform: Windows, macOS, Linux): Paid license; free trial available

: Visual thinkers, dev teams, multi-database projects, Git-based workflows

Personal note: One of the easiest tools to use. You can sketch ideas and build models fast without needing to connect to a database.

A clean, browser-based tool with a professional feel, Vertabelo is a favorite among architects who care about model-first workflows.

  • Build logical models independent of any database engine
  • Support for data type mapping and validation rules
  • Naming conventions and metadata descriptions
  • Comment-based model documentation
  • Logical-to-physical model conversion when needed

: Web-based application: Paid plan; read-only viewer is free; free trial available

: Cloud-based teams, architect-led modeling, precise structure-first designs

Personal note: Clean and professional interface. Great for architects. Just know that the good stuff is behind a paywall.

ER/Studio offers deep modeling capabilities with a strong focus on consistency and documentation.

  • Support for logical models with business glossaries
  • Enforce naming templates and data domains
  • Logical-to-physical conversion tools
  • Git integration and collaborative modeling
  • Extensive metadata documentation capabilities

: Desktop application (Windows): Paid license; free trial available

: Large-scale data initiatives, collaborative model governance

Personal note: Great if you’re working in a team and need consistency. It’s solid, but not very beginner-friendly.

Despite its branding, it’s surprisingly agnostic when it comes to pure logical modeling.

  • Design logical models with detailed entity relationships
  • Apply naming standards and transformation rules
  • Perform validation and integrity checks on models
  • Logical/relational/physical layer separation

: Desktop application (cross-platform: Windows, macOS, Linux): Free

: Oracle shops, rules-driven modeling, transformation-heavy environments

Personal note: Honestly underrated. It’s free, and while the interface feels a bit old, it gets the job done surprisingly well.

PowerDesigner is a bit of a beast. It’s powerful, but not exactly light.

  • Conceptual and logical modeling support
  • Metadata dictionaries and naming transformation rules
  • Dependency and impact analysis tools
  • Reusable model components for large-scale projects

: Desktop application (Windows): Paid license; free trial available

: Enterprise architects, long-term modeling roadmaps, SAP-heavy orgs

Personal note: This one takes time to learn. Even something basic like adding an entity and attributes can feel slow at first.

Visual Paradigm is a multi-discipline modeling tool that happens to include solid logical database design.

  • Logical ERDs with cardinality and optionality
  • Add metadata, notes, and descriptions
  • Support for object-relational mapping
  • Easy switching between logical and physical design
  • Integrates into broader software design workflows

: Web-based and desktop applications (Windows, macOS, Linux): Paid; free community edition available (limited features)

: Software teams who need both app and data design in one place

Personal note: It was hard to find where is the option to create a logical design, so if you are struggling too: New Project -> System Design-New ERD. However, it’s a good tool if you also need to work on flowcharts or system diagrams. But for just database design, it’s not the fastest.

Logical modeling isn’t just drawing shapes — it’s about helping everyone understand the data. Whether you’re building a small app or a big system, the best tool is the one that helps your team stay on the same page.

Some tools (like ERwin or PowerDesigner) go deep into metadata and governance. Others (like DbSchema or Vertabelo) focus on clean modeling and fast iteration. The right choice depends on your team’s priorities: collaboration, standardization, documentation, or transformation.

Just make sure you’re not skipping logical design altogether-because shortcuts at this stage are often the most expensive mistakes you can make later.

Read more about how to start a logical database design with DbSchema.

items.title: MCP Server Hands-on for DevOps Engineers(3) Enhance MCP Server with vibe coding.
items.author: Shota Takano
items.content:
This is the second in a series of blog posts exploring developping and using MCP server in your WSL2/VSCode with github copilot environment.

This article series shows..

the following architecture in the picture below. I will show you how to develop MCP Server to call api endpoints of zabbix server.

  • DevOps Engineers who want to automate their operations using LLM.
  • Engineers who want to know how to develop MCP Server under WSL2 environment.

This page focuses on how to prepare and improve the MCP Server leveraging the power of AI agent. (Vibe Coding)

  • WSL2(Ubuntu) environment with docker/docker-composeThis article is tested under Ubuntu22.04
  • VSCode with github copilotCheck out if Agent mode is enabled in your copilot chat window.
  • Setting up environment as written in here and here.

First, what you have to do is not writing code but creating plan to improve the MCP server. Assume that you want to increase the functions of this zabbix-mcp-server. While it’s possible to writing each functions by hands, it is so time-consuming work. In general, there are official docs for each software project. For example, zabbix server has a great API document.

The document start with overview (common specs for API).

In addition, there are details of each API endpoints and methods.

These documents are useful for vibe coding.

Step2 Setting up Vibe coding environment

Second step is setting up your AI agent and make it possible to retrieve the API docs and suggest code according to the docs.

There is useful service called Firecrawl to get web pages and there is an official mcp server.(github)

I set up my VS code environment(settings.json) to activate Firecrawl MCP Server as following.Getting API key for firecrawl and Installing npx on your WSL server in advance are needed.

//add there lines "firecrawl": {    "args": [        "-c",    ],      "FIRECRAWL_API_KEY": "<your firecrawl api key>",
      "WSLENV": "FIRECRAWL_API_KEY/u"
    }

Now you are ready to vibe code!

In this step, I will show you modify the previous python script and make common components for calling api such as HTTP header difinition move out of the get_host function.

I backed up my python script.

$ mv zabbix-mcp-server-v0.py zabbix-mcp-server-v1.py

And ask github copilot as below.

Note that you are opening zabbix-mcp-server-v1.py in the active window.

I'd lile to modify my python script and add functions to call various zabbix API methods.Move the common component outside the get_hosts_from_zabbix function according to the official document(url is shown as following.)https://www.zabbix.com/documentation/current/en/manual/api

If you are asked to use fetch web page, firecrawl mcp server is successfully activated.

AI agent will say that it modified your code.

In my case, script is modifyed like following.

import osimport requests
from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP
# Initialize FastMCP server
mcp = FastMCP("zabbix-mcp")
# Get Zabbix URL and API token from environment variables
ZABBIX_URL = os.getenv("ZABBIX_URL")
API_TOKEN = os.getenv("API_TOKEN")
# Define a common function to interact with the Zabbix API
def call_zabbix_api(method, params):    Generic function to call Zabbix API methods        method (str): Zabbix API method to call
        params (dict): Parameters for the API method
        dict: Response from the Zabbix API    headers = {
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {API_TOKEN}"        "jsonrpc": "2.0",        "params": params,    }
    response = requests.post(ZABBIX_URL, headers=headers, json=payload)
    if response.status_code != 200:
        print(f"HTTP error: {response.status_code}")
        print(response.text)        print("Zabbix API error:")        exit(1)
# Update the get_hosts_from_zabbix function to use the common component
@mcp.tool()
def get_hosts_from_zabbix():
    """
    Function to retrieve host information from the Zabbix server
        str: List of hosts    params = {
        "output": ["hostid", "host", "name"]
    }
    result = call_zabbix_api("host.get", params)
    for host in result:
        print(f"{host['hostid']}: {host['host']} ({host['name']})")
    return str(result)  # Convert to string and return
if __name__ == "__main__":
    mcp.run(transport="stdio")

Step3 Adding more functions to MCP Server

In the last section, add multipul MCP functions according to the API docs.

I backed up my python script again.

 $ cp zabbix-mcp-server-v1.py zabbix-mcp-server-v2.py

I will add functions about Alert(totally new) and Host(partially implemented).

Next, ask github copilot.

Five methods are automatically added.

I want to add functions that call zabbix api endpoints as mcp tool.Add codes about "Alert" and "Host" including all methods.
Note that Zabbix Server version is 7.2.
API document is written in following url and child pages.
Crawl all related pages and implement all methods written in the docs.
https://www.zabbix.com/documentation/current/en/manual/api

After that, I checked both script and document and request AI agent to add functions not implemented.

Here is the completed script.

import osimport requests
from mcp.server.fastmcp import FastMCP
# Initialize FastMCP server
mcp = FastMCP("zabbix-mcp")
# Get Zabbix URL and API token from environment variables
ZABBIX_URL = os.getenv("ZABBIX_URL")
API_TOKEN = os.getenv("API_TOKEN")
# Define a common function to interact with the Zabbix API
def call_zabbix_api(method, params):    Generic function to call Zabbix API methods        method (str): Zabbix API method to call
        params (dict): Parameters for the API method
        dict: Response from the Zabbix API    headers = {
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {API_TOKEN}"        "jsonrpc": "2.0",        "params": params,    }
    response = requests.post(ZABBIX_URL, headers=headers, json=payload)
    if response.status_code != 200:
        print(f"HTTP error: {response.status_code}")
        print(response.text)        print("Zabbix API error:")        exit(1)
# Add MCP tools for Alert methods
@mcp.tool()
def get_alerts_from_zabbix():
    """
    Function to retrieve alert information from the Zabbix server
        str: List of alerts    params = {    }
    result = call_zabbix_api("alert.get", params)
    for alert in result:
        print(f"Alert ID: {alert['alertid']}, Message: {alert.get('message', 'N/A')}")
    return str(result)  # Convert to string and return
# Add MCP tools for Host methods
@mcp.tool()
def create_host_in_zabbix(host_data):
    """
    Function to create a new host in the Zabbix server
        host_data (dict): Data for the new host        str: Result of the host creation    result = call_zabbix_api("host.create", host_data)
    print(f"Host created with ID: {result['hostids']}")
    return str(result)def get_hosts_from_zabbix():    Function to retrieve host information from the Zabbix server        str: List of hosts    params = {
        "output": ["hostid", "host", "name"]
    }
    result = call_zabbix_api("host.get", params)
    for host in result:
        print(f"{host['hostid']}: {host['host']} ({host['name']})")
    return str(result)  # Convert to string and returndef update_host_in_zabbix(host_data):    Function to update an existing host in the Zabbix server        host_data (dict): Data for updating the host        str: Result of the host update    result = call_zabbix_api("host.update", host_data)
    print(f"Host updated with ID: {result['hostids']}")
    return str(result)def delete_host_from_zabbix(host_ids):    Function to delete hosts from the Zabbix server        host_ids (list): List of host IDs to delete        str: Result of the host deletion    params = {    }
    result = call_zabbix_api("host.delete", params)
    print(f"Hosts deleted with IDs: {result['hostids']}")
# Add MCP tools for batch operations on hosts
@mcp.tool()
def massadd_hosts_in_zabbix(hosts_data):
    """
    Function to add multiple hosts in the Zabbix server
        hosts_data (list): List of host data dictionaries        str: Result of the mass addition    result = call_zabbix_api("host.massadd", hosts_data)
    print(f"Hosts added with IDs: {result['hostids']}")
    return str(result)def massupdate_hosts_in_zabbix(update_data):    Function to update multiple hosts in the Zabbix server        update_data (dict): Data for updating the hosts        str: Result of the mass update    result = call_zabbix_api("host.massupdate", update_data)
    print(f"Hosts updated with IDs: {result['hostids']}")
    return str(result)def massremove_hosts_from_zabbix(remove_data):    Function to remove multiple hosts from the Zabbix server        remove_data (dict): Data for removing the hosts        str: Result of the mass removal    result = call_zabbix_api("host.massremove", remove_data)
    print(f"Hosts removed with IDs: {result['hostids']}")
    return str(result)
if __name__ == "__main__":
    mcp.run(transport="stdio")

Finally, let’s test the new mcp server!

Build the docker image with v2 tag.

docker build -t zabbix-mcp-handson:v2 .

And modify settings.json.You can restart the mcp server through VS code editor.

Some methods are seem to be correct.

But some methods are to be modified.

For example, deleting host is failed.

It is because API interface is incorrectly implemented in python script.

🚪 Exposing Your Kubernetes App with NodePort — The Easy Guide!

When you deploy an application in Kubernetes, your Pods are created inside the cluster. But how do you make your app accessible the cluster? 🤔

Well, Kubernetes gives us a few ways to expose our app. In this post, we’ll focus on one of the most common and beginner-friendly methods — !

🎯 What is NodePort in Kubernetes?

NodePort is a in Kubernetes that allows you to expose your application to the  — right through the IP address of any and a fixed port.

You can think of it like this:

🧭 ← exposed at → http://<NodeIP>:<NodePort>

🛠️ Step-by-Step: How NodePort Works

Let’s break this down simply:

Your defines your application — including the container port it listens on.

apiVersion: apps/v1kind: Deployment  name: myapp-deployment  replicas: 2    matchLabels:  template:      labels:    spec:      - name: myapp        ports:

Now let’s expose this app using a NodePort:

apiVersion: v1kind: Service  name: myapp-service  type: NodePort    app: myapp    - port: 80           # ClusterIP service port

      nodePort: 30087    # Port exposed on each Node (must be in range 30000–32767)
kubectl apply -f deployment.yamlkubectl apply -f service.yaml

4️⃣ What Happens Behind the Scenes?

🔹 Your is watched by (a component running on every worker node). Kube-proxy:

  • Creates

  • Maps that NodePort → Service Port (80) → Pod Port (8080)

If any of your nodes has a public IP (or internal IP in a private network), you can now access your app via:

You don’t need the Pod’s IP anymore — Kubernetes handles routing for you.

| Name             | Defined Where      | Purpose                            || -----------------|--------------------|----------------------------------- |
|     targetPort   |In Service YAML   | Port inside your Pod/container       |
|     port         |In Service YAML   | Internal port exposed by the Service |

🧠 Key Concepts to Remember

  • For production-level use, we often combine NodePort with or use for more advanced routing.
Client Request → NodeIP:30087              → NodePort → ClusterIP Service (port 80)

NodePort is like a simple gate to let the outside world talk to your app. It’s easy to set up and understand — perfect when you’re learning Kubernetes or running internal tools.

items.title: Security Footage (CTF) — TryHackMe Writeup
items.author: z0diac
items.content:
Security Footage (CTF) — TryHackMe Writeup

In this Capture The Flag (CTF) walkthrough, we explore the “Security Footage” challenge on TryHackMe. The challenge focuses on analyzing network traffic and extracting streaming video frames from a captured .pcap file. By identifying a stream containing JPEG images, we extract the frames and examine them in sequence to uncover hidden flag. This write-up demonstrates the importance of network traffic analysis, stream manipulation, and data extraction techniques in real-world security scenarios.

Room Link: https://tryhackme.com/room/securityfootage

Scenario

Initial Investigation

First of all, I downloaded the .pcap file provided by TryHackMe.

I then opened the .pcap file with Wireshark to begin inspecting the network traffic. I saw that there were only 1109 packets.

After that, I went to Statistics > Protocol Hierarchy to get an overview of the types of traffic captured in the file. I found that there was one HTTP protocol stream, while the rest consisted mostly of TCP traffic.

Next, I navigated to Statistics > Resolved Addresses to check the MAC addresses involved in the capture. I found interesting MAC address, 08:00:27, which resolved to PCS Systemtechnik GmbH.

Then I looked up PCS Systemtechnik GmbH on Google and found that it’s a German manufacturer known for producing video surveillance.

Investigating the TCP Stream

Next, I tried to follow the TCP stream to analyze the content of the communication between the hosts.

What’s interesting is that when I followed the TCP stream, I saw an HTTP response with a Content-Type of multipart/x-mixed-replace and a boundary=BoundaryString. This means the server is continuously sending parts of data (in this case, images) separated by the string --BoundaryString. Each part has a Content-type: image/jpeg, which indicates that the server is likely streaming a video feed made up of JPEG frames.

So, what I did next was search for the term “image” in the TCP stream, and I found multiple instances of image/jpeg.

Extracting the JPEG Images

So after identifying the image/jpeg content in the TCP stream, I had an idea to extract these images.

I decided to save the raw data from the stream. To do this, I exported the stream as a raw file from Wireshark and saved it as stream.raw.

To automate the extraction, I used the following Python script:

import re

def extract_jpegs(raw_file, output_dir):
    with open(raw_file, 'rb') as f:
        data = f.read()
    # Split by boundary
    parts = data.split(b'--BoundaryString')
    for i, part in enumerate(parts):
        if b'JFIF' in part:  # Check for JPEG data
            # Extract JPEG content after headers
            jpeg_start = part.find(b'\xFF\xD8')
            jpeg_end = part.rfind(b'\xFF\xD9') + 2
            if jpeg_start != -1 and jpeg_end != -1:
                jpeg_data = part[jpeg_start:jpeg_end]
                with open(f'{output_dir}/frame_{i}.jpg', 'wb') as img:
                    img.write(jpeg_data)

extract_jpegs('stream.raw', 'frames')

===============================================================
Code Analysis
First, the script opens the raw file in binary mode and reads its contents into memory. The data is then split based on the boundary string --BoundaryString, which separates each image frame in the multipart stream.

For each part of the split data, the script checks if it contains JPEG content. This is done by searching for the “JFIF” marker, which is common in JPEG files. If JPEG data is detected, the script proceeds to locate the start and end of the image by searching for the specific JPEG markers: \xFF\xD8 to indicate the start and \xFF\xD9 to indicate the end. These markers are essential for identifying the exact boundaries of each JPEG image within the raw stream.

Once the start and end of the JPEG data are identified, the script extracts the image content and writes it to a new file. Each image is saved with a unique name, such as frame_1.jpg, frame_2.jpg, and so on, depending on its order in the stream.
===============================================================

After preparing the script, I created a new directory called frames to store the extracted images. Once the directory was set up, I executed the script, which automatically processed the raw stream and saved each extracted JPEG frame into the frames folder.

I navigated to the frames directory where all the JPEG files were stored. Each file represented a frame from the video stream. To uncover the flag, I began inspecting the frames one by one, in a stop-motion-like manner.

Conclusion

This challenge demonstrated how network traffic can be a rich source of valuable information when analyzed correctly. By inspecting the .pcap file in Wireshark, I identified an HTTP stream that contained a video feed in the form of JPEG frames. Using a Python script, I extracted these frames and saved them in a new directory.

After opening each frame individually, I was able to locate the hidden flag embedded within the images. This exercise underscores the importance of securing video streams and monitoring network traffic for sensitive data, as even seemingly innocent data streams can be leveraged for malicious purposes if improperly secured.

LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/muhammad-kahfi-darmawan/
TryHackMe: https://tryhackme.com/p/Kuahfi

Security Footage (CTF) — TryHackMe Writeup was originally published in DevOps.dev on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

items.title: 3 Easy Ways to Restore a PostgreSQL Database in Docker (CLI, Volumes, and pgAdmin)
items.author: Worapon Asavanik
items.content:
Restoring a PostgreSQL database is something every backend developer, data engineer, or DevOps professional needs to do sooner or later. Whether you’re recovering from a crash, cloning an environment, or just moving data between systems, choosing the right restore method can save you time and headaches.

This guide focuses on restoring PostgreSQL databases running inside Docker containers.
In this article, I’ll walk you through three practical ways to restore a PostgreSQL database:

  • Mapping a volume and initializing PostgreSQL in a fresh container
  • Using a .sql file with the Docker CLI (the most practical method)
  • Restoring from a custom format backup via pgAdmin

Each method comes with step-by-step examples and is designed for different real-world scenarios. Whether you’re working within a Docker-based project or just experimenting with local backups, you’ll find a solution that fits.

This article covers:
1.Prerequisites
2.Basic understanding of volumes — A quick overview of bind mounts, Docker-managed volumes, and tmpfs mounts. (Skip this section if you’re already familiar.)
3.Creating a .sql backup file
4.Method 1: Mapping a volume and initializing PostgreSQL in a fresh container
5.Method 2: Using a .sql file with the Docker CLI (Most Common and Practical)
6.Method 3: Restoring from a custom format backup via pgAdmin
7.Summary of all commands

1.Prerequisites

Docker: Docker installed on your system — either Desktop or Linux is fine
PC Version: How to Install Docker on PC
Linux Version: How to Install Docker on Linux

Docker Compose, PostgreSQL, and pgAdmin are required. If you haven’t set them up yet, check out this guide:PostgreSQL Setup 101 for step-by-step instructions using Docker containers.

2.Basic understanding of volumes — A quick overview of bind mounts, Docker-managed volumes, and tmpfs mounts.

The easiest way to understand a volume is to think of it as a shared folder between your computer and a Docker container — perfect for moving data in or out.

To support this, Docker provides three types of mounts:

  1. Bind Mount
  • Links a specific directory or file from your host machine to a path inside the container
  • Great for development (e.g., real-time file editing)
  • Path is fully controlled by you

For example,

volumes:
  - ./src:/app # depends on your directory structure.

In my case, the docker-compose.yml file uses a bind mount with the following path:

./database/chicCRMDB:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d

docker-entrypoint-initdb.d is a special path used by the official PostgreSQL Docker image. Any .sql or .sh file placed in this directory will be executed automatically when the container starts for the first time (i.e., during database initialization).

We’re going to use this mechanism in Method 1 to automatically create our database from a script.

2. Volume (Docker-managed)

  • Docker manages the storage location internally (e.g., /var/lib/docker/volumes/...)
  • Recommended for production use, like databases
  • Easy to share across containers, backup, and move

For example,

services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:15
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data  # Named volume managed by Docker

volumes:
  pgdata:

The docker-compose.yml file will create a path on the host system at /var/lib/docker/volumes/pgdata/_data, while inside the Docker container, the path is /var/lib/postgresql/data.

3. tmpfs Mount

  • Data is stored in RAM, not on disk
  • Fastest option, but data disappears when the container stops
  • Ideal for temporary or sensitive data (e.g., session cache)

For example,

services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:15
    environment:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: example
    tmpfs:
      - /var/lib/postgresql/data

NOTE: ❌ You should not use tmpfs with PostgreSQL if you need to persist data.
PostgreSQL requires persistent storage to retain database files, while tmpfs stores everything in memory — meaning all data will be lost once the container stops.

3.Creating a .sql backup file

Since both Method 1 and Method 2 rely on a .sql file to initialize the PostgreSQL database, let’s create that file first.

Use PuTTY to SSH into the host machine where PostgreSQL is running in a container. the run the following command

sudo docker ps # # List all running containers

Now, let’s run the following command to open a shell inside the running container:

sudo docker exec -it <container_name> sh

In my case:

sudo docker exec -it chiccrmdb-postgresql-1 sh

This command gives you access to an interactive CLI inside the container.

Now, run the following command inside the container to create a .sql backup file:

pg_dump -U <your_username> <your_database> > /<your_volume_directory>/<your_filename>.sql

For example, in my case:

pg_dump -U username database > /docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/database_backup.sql

“This command will export your database into a .sql file and place it inside the mounted volume so that it becomes accessible from the host machine.”

docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/ is a special directory recognized by the official PostgreSQL image. Files placed here will be executed when the container starts for the first time.

After that, navigate to your volume directory to verify the file:

cd <your-volume-directory>
# in my case
cd docker-entrypoint-initdb.d

You should see your .sql file there, e.g., database_backup.sql.

Alternatively, if you don’t want to enter the container, you can run the following command directly from your terminal:

sudo docker exec <container_name> pg_dump -U <your_username> <your_database> > /path/on/host/<your_filename>.sql

4.Method 1: Mapping a volume and initializing PostgreSQL in a fresh container

Let’s access PostgreSQL via pgAdmin 4. As shown in the picture below, I currently have only one table — this is due to the test-regis-login.sql file.

Now I’m going to remove that SQL file:

rm test-regis-login.sql

After removing the old .sql file, don’t forget to delete your PostgreSQL container. Otherwise, Docker won’t run the init script again.

Just run:

sudo docker stop <your_container_id>
sudo docker rm <your_container_id>
# in my case
sudo docker stop 31d
sudo docker rm 31d

Then, run the following command again in the directory where your docker-compose.yml is located:

sudo docker compose up -d

We do this because PostgreSQL uses the docker-entrypoint-initdb.d volume to initialize the database when the container starts for the first time.

Now go back to pgAdmin, then head over to:
Databases > your_database > Schemas > public > Tables
You’ll see that all your tables are already restored — like in the screenshot below.

5.Method 2: Using a .sql file with the Docker CLI (Most Common and Practical)

First, with this Docker CLI method, make sure you have a .sql backup file ready and placed in the correct volume.
Then, navigate to the directory on the host system (not the Docker container path) where the .sql file is stored.

In my case, the backup file database_backup.sql is located at:

dockerCompose/database/chicCRMDB

Now, run the following command to restore the database using Docker CLI:

cat database_backup.sql | docker exec -i <postgresql_container> psql -U <myuser> -d <mydatabase>
  • <postgresql_container> = the ID of your PostgreSQL container
  • <myuser> = your PostgreSQL username
  • <mydatabase> = the target database you want to restore the tables into

“NOTE: This command performs a restore into an existing database — it does not create a new one. Therefore, make sure all three values are correctly defined.”

In my case:

sudo cat database_backup.sql | sudo docker exec -i 51907fe3069e psql -U username -d database

If the command runs successfully, you’ll see output like:

After the command finishes running, head back to pgAdmin.
You will see that all tables from database_backup.sql have been successfully restored.

“Don’t forget to clear your existing tables before restoring a .sql file. If you skip this step, you may run into errors like “table already exists” during the restore process.”

To avoid this issue:

  • Method 1: Remove the existing PostgreSQL container before re-running docker compose up -d.
  • Method 2: Make sure the target database is empty before restoring the .sql file.

6.Method 3: Restoring from a custom format backup via pgAdmin

Backup Process:

Restore Process:

Navigate to:

Databases > postgresql > Schemas > public > Tables

You should now see all the tables restored successfully.

7.Summary of all commands

sudo docker ps # # List all running containers
sudo docker exec -it <container_name> sh
sudo docker exec -it chiccrmdb-postgresql-1 sh
pg_dump -U <your_username> <your_database> > /<your_volume_directory>/<your_filename>.sql
pg_dump -U username database > /docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/database_backup.sql
cd <your-volume-directory>
cd docker-entrypoint-initdb.d
sudo docker exec <container_name> pg_dump -U <your_username> <your_database> > /path/on/host/<your_filename>.sql
rm test-regis-login.sql
sudo docker stop <your_container_id>
sudo docker rm <your_container_id>
sudo docker stop 31d
sudo docker rm 31d
sudo docker compose up -d
cat database_backup.sql | docker exec -i <postgresql_container> psql -U <myuser> -d <mydatabase>

Every clap means a lot and motivates me to keep sharing.
Love you all, readers. Bye-bye! 💙
